confirmed.title: tcp_identify rc!=0 aborts the whole plan, discarding successful tcp_discovery AND skipping udp_identify (sibling of ISSUE-001)
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:724-727 (execute_auto), fail_plan at :692
confirmed.impact: When tcp_identify exits non-zero (very plausible: AUTO_TCP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS uses --version-all/intensity-9 which the code's own comment at :62-67 warns can make nmap die on chatty services, plus --script with --script-timeout 10s where a single NSE crash can yield a non-zero rc), the entire plan is marked 'failed' and execute_auto RETURNS. The completed tcp_discovery results are excluded from import (manifest filters returncode==0 only), and udp_identify for this batch is NEVER attempted. A single noisy TCP service thus wipes out both the open-port list and all UDP exposure data. This is the same class as ISSUE-001 but for the TCP-identify stage and it additionally prevents the later UDP stage from running at all.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Treat each stage failure as a per-stage/per-batch error: record the failed stage, continue to udp_identify and to remaining batches, and only mark the overall plan 'partial'/'completed_with_errors' at the end. Do not 'return fail_plan' from inside the stage; accumulate failures and let finish_plan run so good XML is still manifested.

verdict.rationale:
Confirmed in scanner/scanops_scanner.py. At lines 724-727 of execute_auto, when tcp_identify returns rc!=0 the code does `return fail_plan(plan, state_path, rc)`. fail_plan (lines 692-697) sets status="failed", writes the manifest, and returns WITHOUT calling finish_plan — and because it returns out of execute_auto, it exits the per-batch `for idx` loop at line 712. Consequences I verified: (1) udp_identify for the current batch (lines 737-763) is never reached, so UDP exposure data for that batch is lost; (2) plan["cursor"] (line 765) is never advanced and all remaining batches are never scanned; (3) the failed tcp_identify run is excluded from both manifest lists because write_manifest filters returncode==0 (lines 627, 634), so the open-port/service data from that batch is dropped from import.

The non-zero-rc precondition is plausible: AUTO_TCP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS (lines 64-67) uses --version-all (intensity 9), --script DEFAULT_NSE_SCRIPTS, and --script-timeout 10s; the code's own comment at lines 62-63 explicitly notes services that "die" under port scanning, and any NSE/version-probe fault that yields a non-zero nmap exit will trigger this path.

This is a genuine sibling of ISSUE-001 (which concerns the udp_identify stage at lines 748-749/757-758/762-763) and is strictly worse: a tcp_identify failure additionally prevents the later UDP stage from ever running for that batch and aborts all subsequent batches. The identical `return fail_plan` antipattern also appears at lines 716-717 (tcp_discovery), 748-749, 757-758, and 762-763, so the correct fix is structural: treat each stage failure per-batch — record it, continue to udp_identify and remaining batches, and only finalize with a partial/completed-with-errors status via finish_plan so the good XML is still manifested.

Two minor corrections to the finding's wording: the proposed-fix line citation should be 724-727 (the tcp_identify call), not the udp lines; and "tcp_discovery results are excluded from import" is imprecise — tcp_discovery XML is excluded from import_xml_files BY DESIGN (line 634, stage_id != 'tcp_discovery') even on success, though it does appear in all_xml_files when rc==0. The substantive impact (lost open-port import data for the batch + UDP never run + remaining batches skipped + plan marked failed) is accurate. Severity is high rather than critical: results are not corrupted, the failed batch's outputs still exist on disk in plan['runs'].files, and a re-run can resume from cursor; the damage is silent data loss/abort within a run, not destruction.

confirmed.title: Partial-but-valid XML from a non-zero-exit stage is written to disk but silently excluded from the import manifest
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:624-637 (write_manifest), produced files recorded at :685 (run_nmap_stage)
confirmed.evidence:
run_nmap_stage records files unconditionally: "files": existing_outputs(base) at :685 (regardless of rc).
write_manifest then filters them out: all_xml_files / import_xml_files both require `if run.get("returncode") == 0 and not run.get("skipped")` (:627, :634).
confirmed.impact: nmap routinely exits non-zero on RECOVERABLE conditions (e.g. host becomes unreachable mid-scan, an NSE script raising, '0 hosts up') while still writing a complete, useful XML for the hosts it did scan. Because the manifest's import list keys purely off returncode==0, that XML — which physically exists in output_dir and even ends up in the zip via create_zip (:645 iterates all runs' files) — is invisible to the ScanOps import path. Operators get a zip whose manifest omits real findings, so exposed ports go unreported.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Decide inclusion based on whether a parseable XML with host data was produced, not solely on rc. e.g. include a run's .xml in import_xml_files when ET.parse succeeds and it contains at least one <host>, even if rc!=0; optionally tag it as partial. Reuse the existing open_ports_from_xml/live_hosts_from_xml parse guards.

verdict.rationale:
Confirmed against scanner/scanops_scanner.py. run_nmap_stage records "files": existing_outputs(base) unconditionally at :685 and appends the run (with its non-zero returncode from :682) to plan["runs"] at :687 before returning rc. write_manifest then gates BOTH all_xml_files (:627) and import_xml_files (:634) on run.get("returncode") == 0, so a stage that exits non-zero but wrote a parseable, host-bearing XML is excluded from the import manifest. This path is genuinely reachable: every rc!=0 triggers an immediate return fail_plan(...) (execute_auto :717/:727/:749/:758/:763, execute_single :704), and fail_plan calls write_manifest(plan) at :696 with the failed run present. nmap commonly exits non-zero while still emitting a complete XML for hosts it scanned (host unreachable mid-scan, NSE script error, 0 hosts up), so real open-port data in output_dir becomes invisible to the ScanOps import list. The parse guards to do this safely already exist (open_ports_from_xml :442 / live_hosts_from_xml :464 both wrap ET.parse and return [] on ParseError). 

Two caveats on the finding as written: (1) the impact's claim that the XML "even ends up in the zip via create_zip" is INCORRECT for the failure path — fail_plan does NOT call create_zip; only finish_plan zips (:660-661), and finish_plan is unreachable while a non-zero run exists. So on failure no zip is produced at all; the XML sits in output_dir unreferenced. (2) Strong overlap with the already-filed ISSUE-001: both stem from the same root cause (non-zero exit -> fail_plan discards usable data). This is an expansion of that root cause (it covers the *failing stage's own* valid XML, plus the manifest-filter mechanism distinct from ISSUE-001's whole-plan-abort framing), not a pure duplicate. Severity medium rather than high: it requires a non-zero exit that nonetheless wrote a valid XML, the operator does see a non-zero CLI exit + status=failed (not fully silent), and it shares remediation with ISSUE-001. Surgical fix: in write_manifest, include a run's .xml when ET.parse succeeds and it contains >=1 <host>, even if rc!=0, optionally tagging it partial.

confirmed.title: One bad host/stage in a single batch aborts ALL remaining batches in a multi-batch run
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:711-766 (execute_auto batch loop) and :700-708 (execute_single)
confirmed.evidence:
for idx in range(int(plan["cursor"]), len(plan["batches"])):  # :712
    ... rc = run_nmap_stage(...)
    if rc != 0: return fail_plan(plan, state_path, rc)  # :716/717 etc — returns out of the batch loop entirely
confirmed.impact: With --batch-size > 0 (the documented mode for scanning many hosts / large CIDRs), batches are independent units of work. A non-zero exit in batch 0 (e.g. one unreachable subnet, or one host running a service that crashes nmap) returns out of the loop, so every later batch is never scanned. The plan is 'failed' even though batches that already finished have good XML, and the bulk of the target range is silently never touched. For internal network exposure scanning this means large blind spots from a single flaky host.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Isolate failures at the batch level: wrap each batch's stages so a failure records the batch as failed and `continue`s to the next batch instead of returning. Advance plan['cursor'] past completed batches and finish with an aggregate status, so resume re-runs only the failed batches.

verdict.rationale:
Confirmed genuine and grounded in code. make_batches (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:230-233) produces multiple batches when --batch-size>0, which is documented (line 825) as the mode for scanning many hosts/large CIDRs. In execute_auto the batch loop `for idx in range(int(plan["cursor"]), len(plan["batches"]))` (line 712) executes `return fail_plan(plan, state_path, rc)` on every non-zero stage rc — at lines 717, 727, 749, 758, 763. Because it is a `return`, the first failing stage in ANY batch exits the whole loop, so all later batches (idx+1..N-1) are never run, and the plan is marked status="failed" (fail_plan, lines 692-697). execute_single has the identical defect (lines 701-708, return at 704). This is a distinct axis from ISSUE-001 (which is about a UDP stage failure discarding TCP results within a single batch); this is about cross-batch failure isolation in a multi-batch run.

Verified impact and one nuance the finding slightly overstates: because fail_plan returns before `plan["cursor"]=idx+1` (line 765) runs, cursor is NOT advanced past the failed batch, so a manual --resume (load_plan 586-598, execute restarts loop at plan["cursor"]) re-runs from the failed batch and, via stage_succeeded, skips already-completed stages. So it is not strictly "silently never touched forever" — recovery is possible. BUT: (1) the single invocation does silently stop at the first bad batch and report failure; (2) a host that persistently makes nmap exit non-zero (the finding's own example) sits in an early batch and aborts again on every resume, so the entire later range stays permanently unscanned without manual surgery; (3) this defeats the purpose of batching independent units of work. For internal network exposure scanning, one flaky subnet/host causing the bulk of the range to be skipped is a real, high-severity reliability gap.

Worth fixing: the proposed fix (isolate at batch level — record the batch as failed, `continue` to the next batch, advance cursor past completed batches, finish with an aggregate status and resume only failed batches) is the right shape, surgical, and directly serves the stated production-reliability goal. Not speculative gold-plating.

confirmed.title: fail_plan writes manifest with returncode==0 filter, so a mid-plan failure yields a manifest that omits the very stage data already on disk
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:692-697 (fail_plan) calling write_manifest at :696
confirmed.evidence:
def fail_plan(plan, state_path, rc):
    plan["status"] = "failed"
    plan["finished_at"] = now_iso()
    write_json(state_path, plan)
    write_manifest(plan)   # :696 — manifest still applies returncode==0 filter at :627/:634
    return rc
confirmed.impact: On failure, fail_plan DOES emit a manifest (good intent), but write_manifest's returncode==0 + skipped filter means the failing stage's partial XML is excluded, and (when udp/identify failed) the manifest may be near-empty even though tcp_discovery/tcp_identify XML exist on disk. Worse, fail_plan never zips (zip only happens in finish_plan), so an operator who passed --zip gets no zip at all on partial failure and must hand-collect files. The 'failed' manifest is thus a misleading record of what was actually captured.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Have fail_plan (or a unified finalizer) include all parseable XML produced so far and honor --zip even on partial completion, so a failed/partial run still ships a usable bundle. Mark partial entries explicitly rather than dropping them.

verdict.rationale:
Verified in scanner/scanops_scanner.py. fail_plan (lines 692-697) DOES emit a manifest via write_manifest(plan) at :696 (no zip arg). write_manifest filters all_xml_files and import_xml_files to runs with returncode==0 and not skipped (lines 627 and 634). run_nmap_stage records the failing run with returncode=rc (non-zero, :682) and files=existing_outputs(base) (:685, which globs whatever .xml nmap wrote before exiting, see existing_outputs :433-439). Therefore the failing stage's partial XML IS on disk but IS excluded from the manifest by the returncode==0 filter. CONFIRMED. Also confirmed: zipping only happens in finish_plan (create_zip called at :660-661); fail_plan takes no zip_outputs param and never zips, so an operator who passed --zip gets no bundle on partial failure and must hand-collect files. CONFIRMED.

One material correction to the finding's framing: the title ("omits the very stage data already on disk") and the "near-empty manifest" claim are OVERSTATED. Successful prior stages (tcp_discovery, tcp_identify) are recorded as returncode==0 runs and ARE included in the manifest; only the single FAILING stage's partial XML is dropped. So when udp_identify fails, the failed manifest still lists the tcp_discovery/tcp_identify XML (import_xml_files even still lists tcp_identify) — it is not near-empty, just missing the aborted stage. The real, defensible defects are: (1) the failing stage's partial XML is silently dropped from the manifest, and (2) --zip is entirely ignored on partial failure.

Distinct from ISSUE-001: that issue is about the abort decision (whole plan failed/results discarded via the control flow); this is about the quality/completeness of the failure ARTIFACTS (manifest + missing zip). The natural fix — a unified finalizer that honors --zip and marks partial entries explicitly even on failure — overlaps with ISSUE-001's remediation, so it is worth folding in. Note: partial nmap XML from a killed/aborted run can be malformed and may not parse cleanly on import, which slightly tempers (1); (2) is the cleaner operator-facing bug.

confirmed.title: tcp_discovery rc!=0 aborts before any identify even when discovery wrote a usable host/port XML
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:714-717 (execute_auto)
confirmed.impact: Discovery uses -sS raw-socket flags (AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS, :54-59) plus aggressive timing (-T4, --max-parallelism 100). nmap can exit non-zero (e.g. RST ratelimit warnings escalating, a transient send failure) after having already written discovery XML with live hosts and open ports. Because tcp_discovery rc!=0 hard-returns, tcp_identify and udp_identify never run despite a perfectly usable discovery XML existing on disk (open_ports_from_xml/live_hosts_from_xml would parse it). The whole auto pipeline is lost over a discovery exit code that did not actually invalidate the discovery data.
confirmed.proposed_fix: After discovery, decide continuation on the parsed XML (live_hosts / tcp_ports present) rather than on rc alone. If discovery produced parseable host data, proceed to identify stages and record discovery as 'completed with warnings' instead of failing the plan.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed in scanner/scanops_scanner.py:711-768 (execute_auto). At line 715 discovery runs via run_nmap_stage, which builds the command with `-oA` (build_command, line 424) so nmap writes .xml/.nmap/.gnmap during the run (run_nmap_stage, 667-689). Lines 716-717 then hard-return fail_plan(plan, state_path, rc) on ANY non-zero rc, so tcp_identify (723-727) and udp_identify (737-763) never execute. The code immediately below (719-722) proves the discovery XML is independently usable: open_ports_from_xml/live_hosts_from_xml (442-487) parse the on-disk XML with no dependence on rc. There is no fatal-vs-warning distinction anywhere — every stage treats rc!=0 as a plan-killing failure (grep: lines 703, 716, 726, 748, 757, 762). This is the same defect class as ISSUE-001 (a stage's non-zero exit discarding usable results) but distinct and strictly broader: ISSUE-001 is the udp_identify tail stage; this is the discovery HEAD stage, so its abort blocks the ENTIRE identify pipeline and discards already-written discovery data. The fix is surgical and mirrors the author's own intent in ISSUE-001: after discovery, gate continuation on parsed XML (live_hosts / tcp_ports present) rather than rc alone, recording discovery as completed-with-warnings. Caveat lowering confidence slightly: the finding's specific trigger ("RST ratelimit warnings escalating, transient send failure") is somewhat overstated — nmap prints those to stderr but normally still exits 0; nmap reserves non-zero mainly for fatal aborts, where XML may be only partially finalized. But partial-results-on-abort (hosts already flushed before a late fatal, or a finalized-but-nonzero exit) is realistic, and the over-broad `rc != 0` handling is unambiguously present. Severity medium: requires a non-zero discovery exit to manifest (not every run), but when it does the whole auto pipeline is lost over recoverable data — directly counter to the production-reliability goal.

confirmed.title: GUI surfaces non-zero CLI exit purely as a numeric code with no indication that partial results exist
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:398-403 (_drain_output done handling)
confirmed.evidence:
rc = int(payload)
... self.status.set("완료" if rc == 0 else f"종료 코드 {rc}")
self._append_log(f"\nexit code: {rc}\n")
confirmed.impact: Because the CLI escalates any stage failure to a non-zero process exit (fail_plan -> main returns rc), the GUI shows only 'exit code 7' (or 1/2) with no hint that a manifest/zip with usable discovery/identify XML may still have been written. An operator sees 'failure', assumes nothing was captured, and discards a run that actually produced exploitable exposure data on disk. The binary fail-the-plan model in the CLI thus directly degrades the GUI's reliability story.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Once the CLI distinguishes partial from fatal (findings above), have it print/emit a 'partial: manifest written' line on recoverable failure and a distinct exit code; GUI should map that to a 'completed with warnings — results saved' status pointing at the output dir, rather than a bare error code.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed by reading both files. CLI side: fail_plan (scanops_scanner.py:692-697) sets status="failed" and writes the manifest via write_manifest(plan) — so partial discovery/identify XML IS persisted on disk — but returns a bare rc and prints nothing pointing to the manifest. Every stage rc!=0 escalates to fail_plan (lines 717,727,749,758,763), and that rc propagates through execute_auto/execute_single -> execute (785-786) -> main (837) to the process exit code. The "done: {manifest_path}" print (line 662) lives only in finish_plan, which is reached solely on success, so the failure path emits no manifest hint. GUI side (scanops_scanner_gui.py:399-403): rc = int(payload); status.set("완료" if rc==0 else f"종료 코드 {rc}"); _append_log("exit code: {rc}") — purely numeric, with no indication that a manifest/partial results exist. Net effect: an operator sees only "종료 코드 7" / "exit code: 7" and reasonably assumes nothing was captured, discarding a run whose manifest and nmap XML are actually on disk. This is real and in-scope for standalone-scanner UX/reliability. Distinct from ISSUE-001 (which is about the CLI discarding results) — here the data IS written, but neither CLI output nor GUI signals it. Severity low: presentation/signaling gap, not data loss. worth_fixing=true but with a caveat: the full proposed fix (CLI emits a distinct partial exit code + "manifest written" line; GUI maps it to "completed with warnings — results saved" pointing at the output dir) is entangled with ISSUE-001's partial-vs-fatal redesign. A small standalone GUI-only mitigation (on non-zero rc, surface the output dir and note a manifest may exist) is justified and surgical for production use.

confirmed.title: No nmap --host-timeout and no subprocess timeout: a single host can hang the whole scan forever
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:674 (run_nmap_stage: subprocess.call(cmd, shell=False)); flag sets AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS:54-59, AUTO_TCP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS:64-67, AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS:72-76
confirmed.evidence: rc = subprocess.call(cmd, shell=False)  # line 674, no timeout= argument. None of the AUTO_* flag lists contain --host-timeout (grep for host-timeout returns only the comment at line 68). A full-port SYN scan uses -p T:1-65535 with -T4 --max-retries 2 but nothing bounds per-host time. UDP identify deliberately forbids --max-scan-delay (comment line 68), so a heavily rate-limited / filtered UDP host backs off indefinitely.
confirmed.impact: Against a firewalled / silently-dropping internal host (very common for exposure scanning), nmap can run for hours on a single target with no upper bound. subprocess.call() has no timeout, so the scanner process blocks indefinitely. The whole plan stalls on one bad host; the user's only recourse is to hard-kill, which then corrupts the state (see other findings). Production scans become unpredictable in wall-clock time.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Add --host-timeout (e.g. 30m configurable) to each AUTO_* flag set so nmap self-bounds per host and exits 0 with partial results; nmap treats host-timeout as non-fatal. Optionally also pass a generous timeout= to subprocess.call as a hard backstop and treat TimeoutExpired by killing the child and recording the stage as timed-out rather than hanging.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed from the actual code. (1) scanner/scanops_scanner.py:674 `rc = subprocess.call(cmd, shell=False)` has no timeout= argument; this is the CLI engine's only nmap invocation point and execute_single (line 700-708) blocks on it synchronously with no watchdog. (2) grep for "host-timeout" returns zero hits in both files — the only timeout flag present is `--script-timeout 10s` (lines 66, 75), which bounds individual NSE scripts, NOT overall per-host scan time; a host whose ports are all silently dropped never reaches script execution, so that flag does not help. (3) AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS (lines 54-59) does a full-port `-p T:1-65535` SYN scan with `-T4 --max-retries 2`; --max-retries bounds retransmits per probe but not total host time, so a filtered/dropping host can take hours. (4) The comment at line 68 confirms `--max-scan-delay` is deliberately forbidden for UDP identify, so a rate-limited UDP host's scan delay backs off without bound, exactly as claimed. The result: against a firewalled/silently-dropping internal host (very common in exposure scanning) the scanner can hang indefinitely on one target with no upper bound and no auto-recovery. The GUI offers a manual taskkill via _stop (scanner_gui.py:380-390) using Popen, but (a) the CLI itself has zero recourse and (b) hard-kill is the only escape even in the GUI. This is genuinely distinct from ISSUE-001 (which is about exit!=0 aborting the plan) — here the scan never returns at all. In scope for stability/reliability of a production internal scanner. Worth a surgical fix: add `--host-timeout` to each AUTO_* flag list (nmap skips over-budget hosts and exits 0 with partial results, treating host-timeout as non-fatal), and optionally a generous timeout= backstop on the subprocess call with TimeoutExpired handling. Minor caveats on the finding text: line numbers for the flag lists are accurate; "--script-timeout" being the only timeout is correctly characterized as insufficient.

confirmed.title: GUI stop (taskkill /F /T) force-kills the scanner, so state.json is left permanently as status=running (zombie state)
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:380-390 (_stop); scanner/scanops_scanner.py:780-792 (execute: status=running set, only KeyboardInterrupt writes status=interrupted)
confirmed.evidence: GUI _stop: subprocess.run(["taskkill", "/F", "/T", "/PID", str(self.proc.pid)] ...) (line 386). The scanner sets plan["status"]="running"; write_json(state_path, plan) at scanner lines 780-781, and ONLY rewrites it to "interrupted" inside `except KeyboardInterrupt` (lines 787-790). taskkill /F is SIGKILL-equivalent: no Python exception is raised, so the except block never runs. Reproduced empirically: after taskkill /F /T the scanner exited with code 1 (not 130) and the state file's status stayed "running" indefinitely.
confirmed.impact: Every time a user clicks the GUI 중지 (Stop) button, the saved state is left as "running" forever. There is no later process updating it. A human (or any downstream tooling) reading the state file cannot distinguish a still-running scan from a stopped one. The interrupted/resume UX the code advertises ("Resume with: --resume ...", scanner line 791) never prints and the status is wrong.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Make GUI stop graceful: on Windows send CTRL_BREAK_EVENT (self.proc.send_signal(signal.CTRL_BREAK_EVENT)) to the CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP child so the scanner's KeyboardInterrupt path runs and writes status=interrupted, with a short wait()+taskkill /F /T fallback only if it doesn't exit. On POSIX use SIGINT (not SIGTERM) for the same reason. Alternatively, have the scanner install a SIGTERM/SIGBREAK handler that writes status=interrupted before exiting.

verdict.rationale: Verified against the actual code. GUI _stop force-kills via taskkill /F /T (scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:386) on Windows and os.killpg(pid, SIGTERM) on POSIX (line 388). taskkill /F is TerminateProcess (SIGKILL-equivalent) and raises no exception in the target; POSIX SIGTERM is also uncaught. The scanner sets plan["status"]="running" (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:780-781) and ONLY rewrites it to "interrupted" inside `except KeyboardInterrupt` (lines 787-790, return 130). I confirmed the scanner has NO `import signal` (imports at lines 8-22) and NO signal.signal handler anywhere, and main() (lines 833-843) installs none. Therefore the force-kill path never reaches any cleanup: status stays "running" permanently. finish_plan writes "done" (655) and fail_plan writes "failed" (693) only on normal completion/error — neither runs. No later writer corrects it: the GUI _drain_output handler (lines 392-406) only updates the in-GUI status label, never state.json. Net effect: every click of the GUI Stop button leaves a zombie state.json with status="running", indistinguishable from a live scan, and the advertised "Resume with: --resume" hint (line 791) never prints. This is distinct from ISSUE-001 (UDP exit aborting the plan). Two minor corrections to the finding: (1) the bug is NOT Windows-only — the POSIX SIGTERM path is equally uncaught and also leaves "running"; (2) the exact observed exit code (claimed 1) is not code-verifiable but is immaterial to the defect. The proposed fix is viable because the child is already launched with CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP (line 367) / start_new_session=True (line 369), so CTRL_BREAK_EVENT (Windows) or SIGINT (POSIX) can be delivered to trigger the KeyboardInterrupt path, with a taskkill/F fallback. Alternatively install a SIGTERM/SIGBREAK handler in the scanner that writes status=interrupted. Severity high (not critical): scan output files already on disk are preserved and resume still works mechanically since load_plan ignores status and resumes from cursor; only the status field is corrupted, but for a production scanner whose state file is the orchestration/audit source of truth this is a real reliability defect on the GUI's primary Stop action.

confirmed.title: GUI sets CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP but never sends CTRL_BREAK, making the scanner's KeyboardInterrupt/interrupted handler unreachable from the GUI
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:366-367 (creationflags=CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP) vs 380-390 (_stop only taskkill); scanner/scanops_scanner.py:787-792 (interrupted handler)
confirmed.evidence: GUI sets kwargs["creationflags"] = getattr(subprocess, "CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP", 0) (line 367). grep for CTRL_BREAK / send_signal / SIGBREAK in the GUI returns nothing — the flag's only graceful-shutdown purpose (allowing CTRL_BREAK_EVENT delivery) is never used. CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP also disables normal Ctrl-C inheritance, so the child cannot receive a console Ctrl-C either. The scanner's `except KeyboardInterrupt -> status=interrupted` (lines 787-790) therefore only ever fires when the scanner is run manually in a terminal, never via the GUI.
confirmed.impact: The whole 'interrupted' / clean-resume design is dead code on the primary (GUI) usage path. Users believe Stop produces a resumable interrupted state; it actually produces a force-killed process and a stuck 'running' state file. Dead complexity that masks the real behavior.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Either (a) actually use the process group: send signal.CTRL_BREAK_EVENT in _stop and rely on the KeyboardInterrupt handler, or (b) drop CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP and add a scanner-side SIGTERM/SIGBREAK handler that records status=interrupted. Pick one coherent shutdown mechanism.

verdict.rationale:
Confirmed by reading both files. GUI scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:367 sets kwargs["creationflags"] = getattr(subprocess, "CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP", 0). The only stop path on Windows is _stop() at line 386: subprocess.run(["taskkill", "/F", "/T", "/PID", ...]) — a force kill of the whole tree. Grep over the GUI for CTRL_BREAK / send_signal / SIGBREAK returns nothing (only `import signal` at line 8 and the POSIX `signal.SIGTERM` at line 388). The scanner records status="interrupted" ONLY in `except KeyboardInterrupt` at scanner/scanops_scanner.py:787-790. A force-kill (taskkill /F) never delivers a Python KeyboardInterrupt, so that handler is unreachable from the GUI. Net effect is real and grounded: GUI "Stop" hard-kills the scanner (and its nmap child run via subprocess.call at line 674) mid-run; the last state write was status="running" (line 781, or a per-stage write_json), so the state file is left stuck at "running" and the advertised resumable "interrupted" state is never produced via the primary UI. This is a distinct defect from ISSUE-001 (which is about UDP exit!=0 -> fail_plan).

Two precision caveats that lower it from high to medium and slightly correct the finding's framing: (1) The title pins causality on CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP ("makes the handler unreachable"). Strictly the handler is unreachable because _stop chooses taskkill /F, not because of the flag — even without the flag, taskkill /F bypasses any in-process signal handler. The flag is dead/unused scaffolding (its graceful-shutdown purpose of allowing CTRL_BREAK_EVENT is never invoked), a symptom rather than the root cause. (2) The same dead-handler problem also holds on POSIX: _stop sends SIGTERM (line 388) to a scanner that installs no SIGTERM handler, so default action = terminate, not KeyboardInterrupt — so "interrupted" is unreachable from the GUI on every platform, which strengthens the "dead code on the primary path" conclusion. Impact is degraded stop/resume correctness and a misleading stuck "running" state file, not data loss or crash — hence medium (arguably medium-low). In scope: this is standalone-scanner reliability/UX of the documented stop/resume feature.

Worth fixing surgically: pick one coherent shutdown mechanism — either send signal.CTRL_BREAK_EVENT (Windows) / SIGINT (POSIX) from _stop and rely on the existing KeyboardInterrupt handler, or drop the unused CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP and add a scanner-side SIGTERM/SIGBREAK handler that writes status="interrupted". Relevant locations: C:\Users\upica\claude\ScanOps\.claude\worktrees\peaceful-heisenberg-498245\scanner\scanops_scanner_gui.py:366-367 and 380-390; C:\Users\upica\claude\ScanOps\.claude\worktrees\peaceful-heisenberg-498245\scanner\scanops_scanner.py:780-792 and the nmap child at line 674.

confirmed.title: No guard against resuming/running a state that is still status=running — two scanners can write the same state file and outputs
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:586-598 (load_plan) and 772-786 (execute); no status check anywhere before re-running
confirmed.evidence: load_plan() validates only plan['tool']=='scanops_scanner' (line 589) and nmap path; it never inspects plan['status']. execute() unconditionally sets status='running' and proceeds (lines 780-786). Because a hard-stopped scan is left as status='running' (see other finding), a user who clicks 재개 실행 (Resume) — or starts a fresh run pointing at the same name/output-dir — gets a second scanner that re-uses the same state_path and -oA output bases (output_base, lines 404-411) with no lock or staleness check.
confirmed.impact: If the original scanner is somehow still alive, or if the user resumes a genuinely-running plan, two processes interleave writes to <name>.state.json (write_json is a plain truncate-write, line 530-531) and overwrite each other's nmap output files, producing corrupted state and lost/garbled results. Even in the common single-process case, the user gets no warning that the state was left 'running' rather than cleanly finished.
confirmed.proposed_fix: On resume, warn (or require --force) if status=='running'; on a fresh run, refuse to clobber an existing state_path whose status is 'running' unless forced. Optionally write a PID/heartbeat into the state and a simple lock file to detect a live concurrent scanner.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed in code. load_plan() (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:586-598) validates only plan['tool']=='scanops_scanner' (line 589) and the nmap path; it never inspects plan['status']. execute() unconditionally does plan["status"]="running"; write_json(...) (lines 780-781) with no precondition on the prior status. write_json (530-531) is a plain non-atomic truncate-write (path.write_text), no lock/temp+rename. output_base (404-411) is deterministic from output_dir+name+target, so any re-run reuses the same -oA bases and the same state_path (597). The "left as running" premise is also verified end-to-end: the CLI only downgrades status to 'interrupted' on KeyboardInterrupt (787-792), but the GUI _stop() (gui:384-390) issues taskkill /F /T (Windows) or SIGTERM to the process group — neither raises KeyboardInterrupt, so a GUI Stop reliably leaves status='running' on disk; the GUI Resume button (gui:176) then calls --resume with no status check. So the defect is genuine and distinct from ISSUE-001 (which is about UDP-stage fail_plan aborting the plan). Skeptical caveats: the headline "two scanners corrupt each other" needs user-driven concurrency (manually launching a second run), which is contrived; the solidly-grounded harm is single-process — after a forced stop the user gets no warning the state was left 'running', and a fresh/resume run silently clobbers prior outputs and state with no staleness guard. That is a real but moderate, recoverable safety/UX gap, so medium overstates it; low is accurate. A surgical guard (refuse or require --force when an existing state_path has status=='running') is justified and cheap.

confirmed.title: Corrupt/truncated discovery XML is swallowed, yielding an empty-but-"done" scan with exit 0 (a host with open ports is reported as zero exposure)
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:445-448 (open_ports_from_xml), 469-471 (live_hosts_from_xml); consumed in execute_auto:719-735
confirmed.evidence:
open_ports_from_xml: `try:
    root = ET.parse(path).getroot()
except ET.ParseError:
    return []` and identically in live_hosts_from_xml. execute_auto then does `tcp_ports = open_ports_from_xml(discovery_xml,'tcp')` (720) and `live_hosts = live_hosts_from_xml(discovery_xml) or None` (722); empty -> append_skipped_stage(tcp_identify, 'no open TCP ports') (729) and append_skipped_stage(udp_identify, 'no live hosts') (753). finish_plan sets status='done'. I reproduced it end-to-end: a discovery XML truncated mid-port (nmap killed mid-write) that still contained `<status state="up"/>` and port 22 produced: exit 0, status='done', both identify stages skipped, manifest import_xml_files=[].
confirmed.impact: If nmap is OOM-killed, disk fills, the process is interrupted after partial XML flush, or any malformed XML is produced, the scanner silently reports a successful scan that found nothing. An internal host actually exposing SSH/RDP/etc. is recorded as having zero open ports and is dropped from the import set. For a production exposure scanner this is the worst failure mode: a broken scan is indistinguishable from a clean network, so real exposure goes unnoticed.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Distinguish parse failure from genuinely-empty. Have open_ports_from_xml/live_hosts_from_xml raise (or return a sentinel) on ET.ParseError instead of []. In execute_auto, treat a discovery whose XML is missing/unparseable as a stage failure (record returncode!=0 / status!='done') rather than a clean skip. Also sanity-check that the discovery .xml exists and parses before deciding 'no ports/no hosts'.

verdict.rationale:
Confirmed in code. open_ports_from_xml (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:445-448) and live_hosts_from_xml (:468-471) both `return []` on ET.ParseError, making a corrupt/unparseable discovery XML indistinguishable from a genuinely empty result. execute_auto consumes these at :720 (tcp_ports) and :722 (live_hosts); empty tcp_ports -> append_skipped_stage(tcp_identify) at :729; empty live_hosts -> skip udp_identify at :752-753. append_skipped_stage records returncode:0, skipped:True (:521-522). finish_plan sets status='done' (:655); write_manifest builds import_xml_files only from non-skipped rc==0 runs (:631-637) so it is empty; execute_auto returns 0 (:769). The GUI keys solely off this exit code and shows '완료' (gui:402), so a broken scan is presented as a clean, successful one. No post-scan validation flags 'discovery saw a live host but identify produced nothing'. This is distinct from ISSUE-001 (nonzero UDP rc aborts whole plan); here it is the inverse — a failure that should abort is silently swallowed.

One correction to the reporter's framing: the OOM-kill / SIGKILL / disk-full subset is largely MITIGATED, because run_nmap_stage records subprocess.call's rc (:674) and a killed nmap usually returns nonzero, hitting fail_plan at :716-717 — those cases fail loudly, not silently. So the impact's 'OOM-killed/interrupted' examples are overstated. However the CORE defect is genuine and reachable: nmap can exit 0 while emitting XML that ElementTree rejects (illegal XML/control bytes in service-fingerprint or NSE script-output fields is documented nmap behavior). In that case rc==0 + ParseError -> [] -> both identify stages silently skipped -> status done, exit 0, import_xml_files=[]. For a production exposure scanner, a real SSH/RDP host reported as zero exposure with a green 'success' is a severe correctness/reliability failure. Severity high rather than critical because the most dramatic kill/OOM scenarios are caught by the rc check; the residual (nmap-exit-0 malformed XML) is narrower but real. Surgical fix: have the two helpers signal parse failure (raise/sentinel) vs genuine-empty, and in execute_auto treat an existing-but-unparseable discovery XML as a stage failure rather than a clean skip.

confirmed.title: "No live hosts" / "no open ports" is reported as a clean rc=0 skip, not honestly surfaced as an empty result
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:728-729, 752-754 (append_skipped_stage); 654-664 (finish_plan); 620-638 (write_manifest)
confirmed.evidence: A valid discovery XML with all hosts `status state="down"` produces `live_hosts_from_xml -> []` and `open_ports_from_xml -> []` (verified), identical to the corrupt-XML case. finish_plan prints only `done: <manifest>` and exits 0; manifest import_xml_files is empty. There is no summary line stating how many hosts were up or how many open ports were found. The whole-plan terminal output for an empty result is byte-for-byte the same shape as a successful one.
confirmed.impact: An operator cannot tell from exit code or stdout whether the scan (a) found a live network with no open ports, (b) found nothing because all targets were down/unreachable, or (c) broke (see finding #1). All three end with exit 0 and 'done'. There is no honest '0 hosts up / 0 open ports' signal, so empty/failed scans are easily mistaken for confirmed-clean networks.
confirmed.proposed_fix: In finish_plan (or after the per-batch loop), aggregate and print a summary: number of live hosts found in discovery, number of open TCP/UDP ports, number of import XML files. Consider a distinct non-zero exit (or explicit status like 'done_no_results') when zero live hosts were found across all batches, so callers/GUI can flag it.

verdict.rationale:
VERIFIED REAL and grounded in the code.

Terminal output: finish_plan (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:654-664) prints only `done: {plan['manifest_path']}` (line 662) and optionally `zip: ...` (line 664). A full grep of every print() in the file (lines 602-839) confirms there is NO line anywhere that reports a count of live hosts or open ports. The only per-run output is the command echo in run_nmap_stage (line 673). So a scan that finds a live network with services and one that finds nothing both terminate with the identical shape: per-stage command lines followed by `done: <manifest>` and exit 0.

All-down equivalence to corrupt-XML confirmed: live_hosts_from_xml returns [] on ET.ParseError (lines 470-471) AND when every host is status state!="up" (lines 476-477). open_ports_from_xml likewise returns [] on ParseError (447-448) and when no port has state="open" (454-456). Identical empty outputs for "all hosts down", "no open ports", and "corrupt XML".

All-down control flow (execute_auto, lines 712-766) confirmed: discovery runs rc=0; tcp_ports==[] -> tcp_identify appended as skipped (line 729); auto_tcp_discovery_ports true but live_hosts empty -> udp_identify appended as skipped (line 753). Both skipped runs carry returncode=0/skipped=True (append_skipped_stage lines 521-523). Plan then reaches finish_plan and returns 0 (line 769).

Manifest emptiness confirmed: write_manifest.import_xml_files (lines 631-637) excludes skipped runs AND tcp_discovery (line 634). With only a discovery run plus two skipped runs, import_xml_files is [] — byte-shape-identical to a real empty network. Exit code is 0 either way.

GUI confirms operator cannot tell: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py never parses XML/manifest for host or port counts (grep for live_host/open_port/summary/manifest returned no matches). _drain_output (lines 398-403) sets status to "완료" (Done) for any rc==0 and just echoes CLI stdout. So the GUI shows the same "Done" for a clean-confirmed network, an all-down/unreachable scan, and (per ISSUE-001) certain partial results.

Distinct from ISSUE-001: ISSUE-001 is a failure-path defect (UDP rc!=0 -> fail_plan aborts whole plan). This is a success-path observability defect: even when everything "succeeds," there is no honest 0-hosts-up / 0-open-ports signal, so an empty or all-down scan is indistinguishable from a confirmed-clean network. For a production internal-exposure scanner this is a genuine operational hazard (false sense of "clean").

Line citations in the finding all check out (finish_plan 654-664, write_manifest 620-638, skip stages 729 and 753). Severity high is defensible given the security-ops misjudgment risk, though a case for medium exists since it is an observability gap rather than data loss/crash. worth_fixing: yes — a surgical fix (aggregate live-host and open-TCP/UDP-port counts from the per-batch discovery/identify XML and print a summary line in finish_plan, optionally a distinct status/exit when zero hosts up) is small, non-speculative, and directly improves production reliability of the result signal.

confirmed.title: protocol_ports misclassifies a bare port that follows a U: token as UDP, silently dropping TCP ports and scanning them as UDP
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:327-347 (protocol_ports), called by auto_tcp_discovery_ports:350-357 and auto_udp_ports:360-365
confirmed.evidence: protocol_ports keeps a sticky `current` protocol from the last `T:`/`U:` prefix. With `--ports U:53,80`: `protocol_ports('U:53,80','T') -> []` and `protocol_ports('U:53,80','U') -> ['53','80']` (verified). So auto_tcp_discovery_ports('U:53,80') -> '' and auto_udp_ports('U:53,80') -> 'U:53,80'. In execute_auto, auto_tcp_discovery_ports()=='' is falsy (713) so BOTH tcp_discovery and tcp_identify are skipped (732-733), and port 80 is scanned as a UDP port.
confirmed.impact: A user who specifies mixed ports as `U:53,80` (intending 80 as TCP, a natural reading) gets: TCP scanning entirely skipped, and TCP port 80 probed over UDP where it will never respond. The user's TCP intent is silently lost with no error. nmap's own `-p` semantics also make a bare port after `U:` belong to UDP, so this is subtle, but the tool gives no warning that TCP ports were dropped.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Reject or warn when ports_override contains a U:/T: prefix followed by bare (unprefixed) ports, or normalize by requiring every segment to carry its protocol prefix in mixed specs. At minimum, when auto_tcp_discovery_ports() returns '' because the override had no T-classified ports BUT the override is non-empty and contains digits, raise a clear error instead of silently skipping all TCP stages.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed by reading and executing the code. protocol_ports (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:327-347) carries a sticky `current` protocol from the last T:/U: prefix, so a bare port following a U: token is classified UDP. Verified empirically: protocol_ports('U:53,80','T')==[] and ('U:53,80','U')==['53','80']; auto_tcp_discovery_ports({'ports_override':'U:53,80'})=='' and auto_udp_ports==' U:53,80'. In execute_auto (713) the empty TCP spec is falsy, so the else at 731-735 appends tcp_discovery AND tcp_identify as SKIPPED, and port 80 is then scanned over UDP via auto_udp_ports. validate_ports/PORTS_RE (97,180-186) accept 'U:53,80' without complaint, and the only protocol_ports guard (551) fires solely under --tcp-only, so in the default auto workflow there is NO warning that all TCP scanning was dropped. The defect is real and reachable (--ports is free text in both CLI and GUI). I downgrade from high to medium for two reasons: (1) the classification is actually consistent with nmap's own -p semantics (bare port after U: is UDP), which the finding concedes, so it is not a misclassification vs nmap; (2) it requires a specific, atypical mixed-spec form. The genuinely defensible defect is the SILENT loss of all TCP coverage with no error in a security-exposure scanner. Surgical fix: when ports_override is non-empty but auto_tcp_discovery_ports() yields no TCP ports in the auto workflow, warn or error (generalize the existing line-551 guard beyond --tcp-only) rather than silently skipping both TCP stages.

confirmed.title: validate_ports accepts malformed specs (lone 'T:'/'U:', empty/double commas) that flow straight into nmap -p as garbage
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:97 (PORTS_RE), 180-186 (validate_ports); used at build_base_flags:301-304 and create_plan:550
confirmed.evidence: PORTS_RE = `^[0-9TUtu:,\-\s]+$` only checks the character class, not structure. Verified: validate_ports('U:')='U:', validate_ports('T:')='T:', validate_ports(',,')=',,', validate_ports('T:80,,U:53')='T:80,,U:53'. build_base_flags then emits e.g. `-p U:` (verified: ['-Pn','-sV','-T4','-p','U:']). Note also `-p U:` is paired with a profile that has no -sU, so nmap is given a UDP port spec with no UDP scan type.
confirmed.impact: Typos like `U:`, `,,`, or `T:80,,U:53` pass validation and are handed to nmap, which will either error out (wasting a scan run / failing the whole plan) or behave unexpectedly. Validation gives false confidence that the port spec is well-formed when it only filters characters.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Tighten validation to reject empty segments and dangling protocol prefixes: split on ',', require each non-empty segment to be a port/range optionally prefixed by exactly one T:/U:, and error on empty segments. Reuse the same parse logic that protocol_ports relies on so validation and consumption agree.

verdict.rationale:
CONFIRMED REAL by reading the code and running it. PORTS_RE = `^[0-9TUtu:,\-\s]+$` (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:97) is a character-class-only check; validate_ports (180-186) just runs PORTS_RE.match and returns ports.replace(" ", ""), so it never validates structure. Empirically verified the exact claims: validate_ports('U:')=='U:', ('T:')=='T:', (',,')==',,', ('T:80,,U:53')=='T:80,,U:53' (also accepts '-' and ':'). Downstream chain confirmed: in --workflow single, build_base_flags:301-304 takes ports=validate_ports(args.ports) and does flags.extend(["-p", ports]) -> verified build_base_flags emits e.g. ['-Pn','-sV','-T4','-p','U:'] and ['...','-p',',,']. build_command:417,423 passes plan["base_flags"] straight into the nmap argv, and execute_single:704 calls fail_plan on any non-zero rc, so a port spec nmap rejects (e.g. `-p ,,`) aborts the whole single-workflow plan. --workflow single (line 807) and --ports/-p (line 812) are real user-facing inputs, so this is reachable.

TWO corrections to the finding's framing (do not change is_real, but matter for the fix): (1) The garbage-to-nmap bug only fully manifests in --workflow single. In --workflow auto, ports are re-derived through protocol_ports (327) / auto_tcp_discovery_ports / auto_udp_ports, which skip empty segments and dangling T:/U: prefixes (verified: protocol_ports(',,','T')==[], auto_tcp(',,')=='' , protocol_ports('T:80,,U:53','T')==['80']). So auto self-heals; single does not. (2) The finding's note that '-p U: is paired with a profile that has no -sU' is correct for the basic profile, reinforcing that the spec is nonsensical, but the core defect is the validation gap itself.

This is a genuine correctness/UX defect: validation advertises that it filters malformed port specs (it raises a helpful Korean error for disallowed chars) but only filters characters, giving false confidence while letting structurally-broken specs reach nmap and (in single workflow) fail the entire run. It is distinct from ISSUE-001 (UDP-stage abort).

confirmed.title: Standalone scanner enforces no SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE allowlist — a copy-and-run footgun that can scan out-of-org networks
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:163-177 (collect_targets/validate_targets) and create_plan:534-583; contrast backend/scanops/scanning/scope.py:57-93 (check_scope/check_raw_scope)
confirmed.evidence: validate_targets only checks a character regex: `bad = [t for t in targets if not TARGET_RE.match(t)]` (line 175). There is zero reference to SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE anywhere in scanner/ (grep -i scope returns no matches in scanner/). The server, by contrast, gates every scan via check_scope(): `if not nets: return ... bad = [h for h in hosts if not _in_scope(h, nets)] ... raise ValueError("허용된 스캔 대역(scope) 밖의 대상입니다...")`. verdict.rationale: Verified against source. validate_targets (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:174-177) only does a character-class check: `bad = [t for t in targets if not TARGET_RE.match(t)]`, where TARGET_RE (line 96) = `^[A-Za-z0-9_.:/\-]+$`. This accepts any IP/CIDR/hostname-shaped token, so a typo like 100.0.0.0/8 or a pasted external CIDR passes. create_plan (534-583) and collect_targets (163-171) contain no allowlist logic; create_plan only does collect_targets -> expand_targets (a --max-hosts cap check, not scope). Grep for scope|SCAN_SCOPE|SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE across all of scanner/ returns zero matches (the only env access is the GUI copying os.environ for encoding at scanops_scanner_gui.py:354). By contrast the server gates every scan via backend/scanops/scanning/scope.py:57-69 check_scope (raises ValueError on out-of-scope hosts), and that module's docstring (lines 3-6) cites the exact 10.0->100.0 typo scenario. The standalone tool is explicitly documented/designed to be copied and run independently (module docstring scanner/scanops_scanner.py:4-6; README.md:40). README.md:57-59 documents SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE as the guard against scanning external ranges, in a general 보안/운영 section, with no caveat that it does not apply to the standalone scanner; scanner/README.md never mentions scope at all. The auto workflow fires an active full-range SYN scan (AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS lines 54-59: -sS -PE ... -p T:1-65535 -T4 --max-parallelism 100), so a mistyped/external CIDR launches an aggressive scan against a third party with no guard. Minor: the finding's cited line range for validate_targets is loose (the regex check is precisely line 175, within the cited 163-177), but every load-bearing claim is confirmed. confirmed.title: Auto workflow reports success (rc=0, GUI shows "완료") even when the scan found nothing importable

In execute_auto (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:711-769), when tcp_discovery finds 0 open TCP ports the tcp_identify stage is recorded via append_skipped_stage (lines 728-729), and when 0 live hosts are found udp_identify is skipped (line 753). append_skipped_stage marks these with returncode:0, skipped:True (lines 521-522). nmap exits 0 for a successfully-run scan that found nothing, so no fail_plan path triggers; execution falls through to finish_plan (line 768) and returns 0 (line 769). finish_plan only writes state and prints `done: {manifest_path}` (line 662) with no result-emptiness check. write_manifest's import_xml_files comprehension (lines 631-637) excludes skipped runs (line 634) and tcp_discovery, so it is [] when both identify stages are skipped. The GUI maps rc==0 -> status "완료" at scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:402, the only status mapping, with no empty-result branch. Net effect: a dead subnet, fully-firewalled host, or mistyped target completes with green "완료"/`done:` and an empty manifest, indistinguishable from a securely-closed target — a false-negative presented as success for an internal-exposure audit. confirmed.title: GUI Stop on Windows force-kills the CLI (taskkill /F), so the scan's interrupted/resume state is never written and exit shows a generic error code
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:380-390 (_stop) vs scanner/scanops_scanner.py:787-792 (KeyboardInterrupt handler) and 780-781
confirmed.evidence: _stop runs `subprocess.run(["taskkill", "/F", "/T", "/PID", str(self.proc.pid)])` (gui line 386) — a hard, unconditional kill, not Ctrl-C/CTRL_BREAK. The CLI's only graceful path is `except KeyboardInterrupt:` (scanner line 787) which sets `plan["status"] = "interrupted"`, writes state, and prints `"interrupted. Resume with: --resume " + str(state_path)` (line 791). taskkill /F never raises KeyboardInterrupt in the child, so that block does not run; the last persisted status is `"running"` (written at line 781 / after each stage). The killed Python process returns a nonzero code, which _drain_output renders as `self.status.set(f"종료 코드 {rc}")` and logs `exit code: {rc}` (gui lines 402-403).
confirmed.impact: After the user clicks 중지 (Stop): (1) the state.json is stuck at status "running" — a zombie state the README claims the system avoids (README.md:62-63), so a later resume / inspection misrepresents what happened; (2) the user is never shown the resume hint that the CLI was designed to print, so the documented resume UX is silently lost on the most common (Windows) path; (3) the GUI shows a scary "종료 코드 1" (looks like a crash) instead of "중지됨". The GUI even created a CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP (gui line 367) enabling CTRL_BREAK, but _stop never uses it.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Send a graceful signal first: on Windows `self.proc.send_signal(signal.CTRL_BREAK_EVENT)` (the process group flag is already set) and only taskkill /F as a timed fallback; on POSIX keep os.killpg with SIGINT (not SIGTERM) so the CLI's KeyboardInterrupt path runs. Also set a 'stopped' flag so _drain_output displays "중지됨" rather than "종료 코드 N".

verdict.rationale: Confirmed by reading the code. GUI _stop (scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:385-386) on Windows runs `subprocess.run(["taskkill","/F","/T","/PID",str(self.proc.pid)])` — a hard TerminateProcess that does NOT deliver Ctrl-C/CTRL_BREAK, so the child cannot raise KeyboardInterrupt. The CLI's ONLY graceful path is `except KeyboardInterrupt` (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:787-792); grep confirms NO signal.signal handler is installed in the CLI at all. Therefore on Stop: (a) plan["status"] stays "running" (last write at scanops_scanner.py:780-781 and per-stage write_json), never flipping to "interrupted"; (b) the designed resume hint print("\ninterrupted. Resume with: --resume "+state_path) at line 791 never fires; (c) _drain_output (gui:399-403) renders the nonzero exit as `종료 코드 {rc}`/`exit code: {rc}` with no "중지됨" distinction, looking like a crash. The project documents the opposite invariant in README.md:62-63 ("interrupted 로 정직하게 표기 ... 좀비 '실행 중' 방지"). Also confirmed CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP IS set at spawn (gui:367) enabling CTRL_BREAK_EVENT, yet _stop never uses it. ADDITIONAL grounded nuance the finding understated: this is cross-platform — POSIX _stop sends SIGTERM (gui:388), and the CLI has no SIGTERM handler, so the KeyboardInterrupt block is also skipped on Linux/macOS; the proposed POSIX fix (SIGINT instead of SIGTERM) is correct and necessary. Severity adjusted from high to medium: no scan data is actually lost (each stage's outputs are written by run_nmap_stage before Stop, and `cursor` is advanced + persisted, so --resume continues correctly regardless of the status string — load_plan at 586-598 doesn't gate on status), so impact is misleading zombie state + lost resume hint + scary exit-code UX rather than data loss or broken resume. Still worth a surgical fix (send CTRL_BREAK_EVENT first / SIGINT on POSIX, taskkill /F only as timed fallback, and a 'stopped' flag for the status label).

confirmed.title: GUI Stop sends SIGTERM on POSIX, but the CLI only handles KeyboardInterrupt — same lost interrupted-state/resume-hint problem off Windows
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:388 (os.killpg(self.proc.pid, signal.SIGTERM)) vs scanner/scanops_scanner.py:787
confirmed.evidence: The non-Windows branch does `os.killpg(self.proc.pid, signal.SIGTERM)` (gui line 388). The CLI installs no SIGTERM handler; its only cleanup is `except KeyboardInterrupt` (scanner line 787), which corresponds to SIGINT, not SIGTERM. Default SIGTERM disposition terminates the process without running the except block.
confirmed.impact: On Linux/macOS scanner hosts, Stop leaves state.json at "running" and skips the resume-hint print, identical to the Windows defect. Since the README pitches the tool as Windows/Linux/macOS (README.md:41), this affects the documented cross-platform path.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Either send signal.SIGINT instead of SIGTERM from the GUI, or register a SIGTERM handler in the CLI that raises KeyboardInterrupt / runs the same interrupted-state cleanup.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed from code. GUI _stop() POSIX branch sends os.killpg(self.proc.pid, signal.SIGTERM) (scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:388); the GUI launches the CLI with start_new_session=True (gui:369), so SIGTERM hits the CLI process group. The CLI installs NO signal handler: main() (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:833-843) and module top register no signal.signal(...), and the only interrupt cleanup is `except KeyboardInterrupt` at scanner:787, which Python raises only for SIGINT, not SIGTERM. Default SIGTERM disposition kills the Python process without running the except block, so on Linux/macOS Stop leaves state.json at status="running" (set at scanner:780-781, never updated to "interrupted") and never prints the resume hint (scanner:791). README.md:41 documents Windows/Linux/macOS support, so the POSIX path is on the advertised surface. This is the POSIX sibling of the Windows taskkill Stop bug (gui:386) and is distinct from ISSUE-001 (UDP-stage exit aborting the whole plan via fail_plan). Severity medium not high: --resume keys off cursor so scans are still resumable in practice; the harm is the stuck "running" status and missing resume hint, a UX/state-integrity defect rather than data loss. Fix is surgical: send signal.SIGINT instead of SIGTERM from the GUI, or register a SIGTERM->KeyboardInterrupt handler in the CLI.

confirmed.title: Resume UX: the only resume hint goes to stderr from the CLI; the GUI gives no resume affordance after a failed/stopped scan and never auto-fills the state path
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:791 (stderr resume hint) and fail_plan:692-697 (no hint at all); GUI resume section scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:171-176, 259-262
confirmed.evidence: On a stage failure the CLI calls fail_plan (scanner lines 692-697) which writes status "failed" and returns rc but prints NO resume guidance — only the KeyboardInterrupt path prints the `--resume` hint (line 791). In the GUI, resume requires the user to manually browse for `*.state.json` via _browse_resume (gui line 259) and click 재개 실행; nothing auto-populates self.resume_path after a run fails, and the GUI never echoes the state-file location (the CLI prints it only in the interrupt case, to stderr which is merged into the log).
confirmed.impact: After the documented common failure (e.g. ISSUE-001's UDP-stage nonzero exit aborting the plan), the user sees `종료 코드 7` in the GUI with zero indication that the partial scan is resumable or where the state file is. The resume feature exists but is effectively undiscoverable at the moment it is needed, so users re-run the whole expensive full-port scan from scratch.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Have fail_plan print the same `Resume with: --resume <state_path>` hint the interrupt path prints. In the GUI, on a nonzero/failed exit, auto-set self.resume_path to the just-run state.json (derivable from output_dir + name) and surface a one-click '재개' prompt.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed against the actual code on every claim. CLI: fail_plan (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:692-697) writes status "failed" + manifest and returns rc but prints NOTHING — no resume hint, no state path. The only place a resume hint is emitted is the KeyboardInterrupt branch at line 791 (`"\ninterrupted. Resume with: --resume " + str(state_path)`, to sys.stderr). I also verified the state path is never printed on any normal path: print_plan (601-617) prints only `output:`/`batches:`; finish_plan (662) prints `done: <manifest_path>`, not the state file. So after a stage failure (e.g. ISSUE-001's UDP nonzero exit), the user has no on-screen indication the partial scan is resumable or where the .state.json lives. GUI: resume is manual-only — _browse_resume (scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:259-262) + the 재개 실행 button (line 176). _drain_output's "done" handler (lines 398-403) on nonzero rc only sets status `종료 코드 {rc}` and logs `exit code: {rc}`; it never sets self.resume_path and never surfaces the state-file location. The GUI never derives the state path itself and only echoes CLI stdout/merged-stderr (stderr=STDOUT at line 359), which on failure contains no path. Net effect: the resume feature is undiscoverable exactly when it is needed, so users re-run the whole expensive full-port scan. This is a genuine UX defect that compounds the already-filed reliability issue. Severity medium: no data loss or crash (partial results ARE saved and resumable), but it defeats the value of an existing recovery feature. Worth fixing: the fix is surgical and grounded — add the same resume hint to fail_plan (mirror line 791), and in the GUI auto-populate self.resume_path on nonzero exit (path derivable from output_dir + name, set at scanner.py:562). Minor caveat on the proposed GUI fix: deriving the state path from output_dir+name requires the GUI to know the actual --name used (the CLI defaults to scan_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS when --name is blank, scanner.py:803), so the most robust GUI implementation is to have the CLI print the state path on failure and parse it from the log, or have the CLI emit the path on a known prefix — but the core finding stands regardless.

confirmed.title: GUI never validates output name / batch-size numeric edge cases or surfaces CLI validation failures distinctly — silent acceptance of empty results from typos
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:325-329 (batch check) and _command 277-337; CLI argparse type=int at scanner/scanops_scanner.py:825-826
confirmed.evidence: The GUI validates batch size only as `batch.isdigit()` (gui line 327), which rejects negatives by treating them as non-digit but gives the generic message "배치 크기는 숫자여야 합니다"; a value like `0` is allowed and silently means 'one nmap run' but the UI label '배치 크기' offers no hint of that meaning. More importantly, all other invalid inputs (bad ports, bad target tokens, max-hosts cap exceeded) are only caught by the CLI subprocess, whose `error: ...` text (scanner lines 838-843, rc=2) is dumped into the scrolling log as plain text with `exit code: 2`; there is no messagebox or status distinction, so a validation rejection looks the same as a generic failure.
confirmed.impact: A user who fat-fingers a CIDR that expands beyond --max-hosts (default 65536) or an invalid port spec gets a Korean `error:` line buried in the log and a red-herring `종료 코드 2`, with no modal drawing attention. Combined with finding #2 (empty success), misconfigured runs are easy to misread as either success or an opaque crash.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Detect rc==2 (CLI ValueError convention) in _drain_output and pop a messagebox with the captured error line, distinct from other failures. Add a tooltip/label clarifying batch size 0 = single run, and pre-validate ports/targets client-side before spawning.

verdict.rationale: Partially confirmed. The accurate core: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:399-403 (_drain_output) collapses every exit code into a single generic "종료 코드 {rc}" status and just _append_log's output as plain text, with NO messagebox for any failure. There is no distinction between rc=2 (CLI ValueError from invalid ports/targets/max-hosts cap) and rc=1 (OSError/runtime). Grounded: CLI main() lines 838-840 returns rc=2 on ValueError; ValueErrors are raised for bad target tokens (line 177), invalid ports (line 185), and max-hosts cap exceeded (line 226). So a config-validation rejection genuinely looks identical to a generic crash, surfaced only as a buried Korean "error:" log line. Also confirmed: batch size is validated only via isdigit() (gui line 327), and "0"/empty silently means a single nmap run (CLI default, line 825) with no UI hint. HOWEVER, the finding's headline claim is misframed and NOT real: the GUI not validating the output name does NOT cause "silent empty results from typos" — the CLI's safe_name (lines 128-130) sanitizes the name (regex substitution + fallback to scan_<timestamp>), so a bad name is silently cleaned, never empty or failing. Severity is low: the error text is always printed to the visible log, so nothing is truly hidden — the defect is only that attention isn't drawn and a validation rejection is indistinguishable from a crash.

confirmed.title: udp_identify falling back to skipped (no live hosts) is reported identically to a real UDP scan with zero findings — control flow hides why UDP coverage was dropped
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:750-754 (live_hosts empty -> append_skipped_stage) vs 743-749 (--udp-all-targets actually scans)
confirmed.evidence: In the default (non --udp-all-targets) path, if discovery returns no live hosts, udp_identify is recorded as skipped with reason "tcp_discovery 에서 살아있는 호스트를 찾지 못했습니다" (line 753) and the plan still finishes rc=0. The skip is only visible by reading state.json runs[].skip_reason — the console prints nothing about it during the run, and finish_plan emits only `done:`. A host that is silent to TCP/ICMP/ACK discovery but exposes UDP-only services (DNS/SNMP/NTP — exactly the kind of internal exposure this tool targets) is silently never UDP-scanned unless the user happened to know to pass --udp-all-targets.
confirmed.impact: For internal exposure scanning, UDP-only hosts (open resolvers, SNMP, NTP amplifiers) are precisely the high-value findings, and they are dropped with no on-screen indication. The user sees a green completion and assumes UDP was covered; the coverage gap is buried in JSON. This is a silent false-negative in the tool's core use case.
confirmed.proposed_fix: When udp_identify is skipped due to no live hosts (but UDP ports were configured), print a visible warning to stdout suggesting --udp-all-targets, and surface skipped-stage reasons in the GUI log/status rather than only in state.json.

verdict.rationale:
Verified against scanner/scanops_scanner.py and scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py. The defect is real and distinct from ISSUE-001.

Confirmed code path: in execute_auto, when discovery ran (auto_tcp_discovery_ports truthy) and live_hosts is empty, udp_identify is recorded as skipped at line 753 with reason "tcp_discovery 에서 살아있는 호스트를 찾지 못했습니다" via append_skipped_stage (lines 510-527), which writes ONLY to plan["runs"] in state.json and prints nothing. The plan then finishes rc=0 (finish_plan, line 707/768) which prints only "done:" (line 662). No console signal that UDP coverage was dropped.

Default-reachability confirmed: auto_udp_ports (line 360-365) returns the default UDP port set when no --ports override is given (UDP_DEFAULT_PORTS, line 26), so the elif chain reaches the live_hosts check (line 750-754) by default. The skip is thus the DEFAULT outcome whenever discovery finds zero live hosts — not an edge case. A host silent to TCP/ICMP/ACK discovery but exposing UDP-only services (DNS/SNMP/NTP) is never UDP-scanned unless the user passes --udp-all-targets (lines 743-749). This is a genuine silent false-negative in the tool's stated core use case (internal exposure scanning).

GUI half confirmed: _drain_output (gui 392-403) only echoes CLI stdout "line" events and sets status to "완료" on rc=0; it never reads state.json, so skip_reason is invisible in the GUI too (the only state.json reference is the resume-file picker, gui line 175/260).

One correction to the finding's framing: the TITLE overstates by saying the skip is "reported identically to a real UDP scan with zero findings." On the console they are NOT identical — a real UDP scan prints a "[x/y] 주요 UDP 서비스 식별 nmap ..." command line via run_nmap_stage (line 673), whereas the skip prints nothing. The skip is actually LESS visible (fully silent), which strengthens rather than weakens the underlying UX defect. The evidence/impact/proposed_fix fields are accurate.

Distinct from ISSUE-001 (whole-plan abort via fail_plan); this is about silent, by-design coverage drop with no user-facing signal. In scope for scanner UX/reliability. Worth a surgical fix: emit a stdout warning when udp_identify is skipped for no-live-hosts while UDP ports are configured (suggest --udp-all-targets), and surface skipped-stage reasons in the GUI log/status. Severity medium: data is preserved in state.json and the dropped coverage is a documented deliberate tradeoff (comments lines 751-754); the gap is purely missing signaling, not data loss.

confirmed.title: GUI 'TCP만'/'단일 실행에 UDP 추가'/NSE toggles silently no-op or conflict depending on mode, with no feedback
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:154-158 (checks), 165-166 (NSE), _command:313-324; CLI semantics scanner/scanops_scanner.py:298 (args.udp only matters when -sU absent) and build_base_flags vs apply_auto_modifiers
confirmed.evidence: The '단일 실행에 UDP 추가' checkbox label itself admits it only applies to single mode, but the UI still lets the user check it in auto mode where --udp is consumed by build_base_flags (only used for single profiles, scanner line 298) and ignored by the auto path (execute_auto/build_auto_flags never read plan['udp']/args.udp). Likewise '단일 실행에서 기본 NSE 추가' (nse_default, gui line 165) is meaningless in auto mode. open_only and include_closed can both be checked simultaneously (gui lines 156-157); the CLI applies include_closed (strip --open) then open_only (re-add --open) so include_closed silently wins/loses depending on order (scanner lines 314-317 / 376-379) with no warning.
confirmed.impact: Users toggling these options in the wrong mode get no effect and no message, leading them to believe they constrained/expanded the scan when they did not (e.g. checking 'TCP만' is honored, but checking '단일 실행에 UDP 추가' in auto mode does nothing). Conflicting open/closed selections produce non-obvious behavior. This erodes trust and can cause an operator to ship a scan that doesn't match their intent.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Disable/grey out mode-irrelevant checkboxes based on the selected run mode (auto vs single), and make open_only/include_closed mutually exclusive radio-style. Add a brief warning when a checked option will be ignored for the current mode.

verdict.rationale:
Verified against code. The core claim holds: the GUI never gates its checkboxes by run mode (`_mode_changed` gui:197-214 only updates label/port-preset text; the Checkbuttons at gui:154-158,165-166 have no state management), and several toggles are genuine silent no-ops in auto mode.

CONFIRMED no-ops in auto mode:
- `--udp` (self.udp): `_command` always appends `--udp` if checked (gui:315-316) regardless of mode. In the CLI `args.udp` is read ONLY in build_base_flags (scanner:298), which only feeds plan["base_flags"]. The auto path builds stage flags via build_auto_flags (used whenever stage_id is set, scanner:417) and applies apply_auto_modifiers (scanner:368-380), which never reads any udp key. So checking "단일 실행에 UDP 추가" in auto mode does nothing.
- `--nse-default` (self.nse_default, gui:165/317-318): args.nse_default is read only in build_base_flags (scanner:310); the auto path uses plan["scripts"] (apply_auto_modifiers scanner:374) and AUTO_*_FLAGS carry their own NSE, so nse_default is ignored in auto.
- open_only and include_closed can both be checked at once (gui:156-157, no mutual exclusion) and both flags are emitted (gui:321-324) with no warning.

There is no feedback anywhere when a checked option is irrelevant for the current mode. This can cause an operator to believe they expanded coverage (e.g. added UDP in auto) when they did not — a real UX/intent-correctness defect for a tool aimed at production internal-exposure scanning, distinct from ISSUE-001 (exit-code abort).

ONE INACCURACY in the finding to note: it claims include_closed "silently wins/loses depending on order." That is wrong. In both build_base_flags (scanner:314-317) and apply_auto_modifiers (scanner:376-379) the order is fixed in code: include_closed strips --open first, then open_only re-adds --open. So open_only deterministically wins regardless of checkbox order. It is still a real conflict (two contradictory options accepted, one silently overridden, no warning), just not order-dependent.

Net: real, in scope, low severity. A surgical fix (disable mode-irrelevant checkboxes per mode, make open-only/include-closed mutually exclusive, and/or warn when a checked option will be ignored) is justified for the production-reliability goal.

confirmed.title: --scan-type connect still emits -sU in auto UDP stage, so the 'no privileges' choice fatally fails the UDP identify
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:267-272 (set_scan_type), :103 (SCAN_TYPE_FLAGS), :72-76 (AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS); GUI scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:48-52 (SCAN_TYPES label '권한 불필요 - TCP Connect')
confirmed.evidence: SCAN_TYPE_FLAGS = {"-sS", "-sT"} and set_scan_type does `flags = [f for f in flags if f not in SCAN_TYPE_FLAGS]; return [mapped, *flags]`. It only ever strips/replaces -sS/-sT and never touches -sU. AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS begins with -sU. Dry-run with --scan-type connect produces: `-sT -sU -Pn -n -sV ... -p U:7,53,...`. -sU always requires raw-socket (root/admin) privileges; it cannot be performed via connect().
confirmed.impact: A user who explicitly picks 'TCP Connect / no admin privileges required' in the GUI still gets a UDP identify stage that requires admin. On an unprivileged host nmap aborts the UDP stage with 'requires root privileges', which (per ISSUE-001's fail_plan path at :748) marks the whole plan failed. The user's deliberate privilege-free choice is silently violated and the scan dies.
confirmed.proposed_fix: When scan_type=='connect' (no raw-socket privilege available), the UDP identify stage must be skipped with an explicit append_skipped_stage reason instead of emitting -sU. Either strip -sU and skip udp_identify when scan_type is connect, or surface an upfront error/warning that UDP scanning is unavailable without privileges.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed from code and empirical dry-run. set_scan_type (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:267-272) strips only SCAN_TYPE_FLAGS = {"-sS","-sT"} (:103) and never removes -sU. AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS (:72-76) starts with -sU. build_auto_flags(udp_identify) (:398) builds from those flags, then apply_auto_modifiers (:369) calls set_scan_type with plan scan_type. Running build_auto_flags({'scan_type':'connect'}, 'udp_identify') yields: -sT -sU -Pn -n -sV --open --reason -T4 --max-retries 2 --script ... -p U:7,53,... — i.e. -sU is still present alongside -sT. The GUI maps "권한 불필요 - TCP Connect" -> connect (gui :48-52) and passes --scan-type connect (gui :310-312), so this is reachable end-to-end. -sU requires raw-socket (root/admin) privileges and cannot run via connect(); on an unprivileged host nmap exits non-zero, which hits the fail_plan path for udp_identify (:748-749, :757-758, :762-763), aborting the whole plan and discarding successful TCP results. This is distinct from ISSUE-001: ISSUE-001 is the generic "UDP exit!=0 -> fail_plan" cascade; this finding is that the user's explicit no-privileges choice is silently violated by still emitting -sU, which deterministically guarantees that failure for exactly the users who opted out of needing admin. Proposed fix is sound: when scan_type=='connect', skip udp_identify with append_skipped_stage (mirroring the existing tcp_only skip at :737-739) rather than emitting an -sU command that cannot succeed.

confirmed.title: Auto discovery stage keeps raw-socket-only probes (-PE/-PA/--defeat-rst-ratelimit) even under --scan-type connect
confirmed.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:49-59 (AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS: -PE, DISCOVERY_PA='-PA80,443,3389', --defeat-rst-ratelimit), applied via build_auto_flags:384-388 then apply_auto_modifiers/set_scan_type
confirmed.evidence: Dry-run with --scan-type connect: `-sT -PE -PS21,22,... -PA80,443,3389 -n -T4 --reason ... --defeat-rst-ratelimit ...`. set_scan_type swaps -sS->-sT but leaves -PE (ICMP echo), -PA (raw TCP ACK ping) and --defeat-rst-ratelimit untouched. These probe types require raw sockets; unprivileged nmap cannot send raw ICMP/ACK and downgrades or warns, and --defeat-rst-ratelimit has no effect without raw sockets.
confirmed.impact: The 'no privileges' path is not actually privilege-clean: discovery loses its ICMP-echo and ACK reachability probes when run unprivileged, so hosts that answer only ICMP or only ACK (the exact cases the -PE/-PA combo at :47-48 was added to catch) are missed, and discovery silently behaves differently than its documented design.
