Surgical fix: have the two helpers signal parse failure (raise/sentinel) vs genuine-empty, and in execute_auto treat an existing-but-unparseable discovery XML as a stage failure rather than a clean skip.

all.title: "No live hosts" / "no open ports" is reported as a clean rc=0 skip, not honestly surfaced as an empty result
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:728-729, 752-754 (append_skipped_stage); 654-664 (finish_plan); 620-638 (write_manifest)
all.evidence: A valid discovery XML with all hosts `status state="down"` produces `live_hosts_from_xml -> []` and `open_ports_from_xml -> []` (verified), identical to the corrupt-XML case. finish_plan prints only `done: <manifest>` and exits 0; manifest import_xml_files is empty. There is no summary line stating how many hosts were up or how many open ports were found. The whole-plan terminal output for an empty result is byte-for-byte the same shape as a successful one.
all.impact: An operator cannot tell from exit code or stdout whether the scan (a) found a live network with no open ports, (b) found nothing because all targets were down/unreachable, or (c) broke (see finding #1). All three end with exit 0 and 'done'. There is no honest '0 hosts up / 0 open ports' signal, so empty/failed scans are easily mistaken for confirmed-clean networks.
all.proposed_fix: In finish_plan (or after the per-batch loop), aggregate and print a summary: number of live hosts found in discovery, number of open TCP/UDP ports, number of import XML files. Consider a distinct non-zero exit (or explicit status like 'done_no_results') when zero live hosts were found across all batches, so callers/GUI can flag it.

verdict.rationale:
VERIFIED REAL and grounded in the code.

Terminal output: finish_plan (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:654-664) prints only `done: {plan['manifest_path']}` (line 662) and optionally `zip: ...` (line 664). A full grep of every print() in the file (lines 602-839) confirms there is NO line anywhere that reports a count of live hosts or open ports. The only per-run output is the command echo in run_nmap_stage (line 673). So a scan that finds a live network with services and one that finds nothing both terminate with the identical shape: per-stage command lines followed by `done: <manifest>` and exit 0.

All-down equivalence to corrupt-XML confirmed: live_hosts_from_xml returns [] on ET.ParseError (lines 470-471) AND when every host is status state!="up" (lines 476-477). open_ports_from_xml likewise returns [] on ParseError (447-448) and when no port has state="open" (454-456). Identical empty outputs for "all hosts down", "no open ports", and "corrupt XML".

All-down control flow (execute_auto, lines 712-766) confirmed: discovery runs rc=0; tcp_ports==[] -> tcp_identify appended as skipped (line 729); auto_tcp_discovery_ports true but live_hosts empty -> udp_identify appended as skipped (line 753). Both skipped runs carry returncode=0/skipped=True (append_skipped_stage lines 521-523). Plan then reaches finish_plan and returns 0 (line 769).

Manifest emptiness confirmed: write_manifest.import_xml_files (lines 631-637) excludes skipped runs AND tcp_discovery (line 634). With only a discovery run plus two skipped runs, import_xml_files is [] — byte-shape-identical to a real empty network. Exit code is 0 either way.

GUI confirms operator cannot tell: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py never parses XML/manifest for host or port counts (grep for live_host/open_port/summary/manifest returned no matches). _drain_output (lines 398-403) sets status to "완료" (Done) for any rc==0 and just echoes CLI stdout. So the GUI shows the same "Done" for a clean-confirmed network, an all-down/unreachable scan, and (per ISSUE-001) certain partial results.

Distinct from ISSUE-001: ISSUE-001 is a failure-path defect (UDP rc!=0 -> fail_plan aborts whole plan). This is a success-path observability defect: even when everything "succeeds," there is no honest 0-hosts-up / 0-open-ports signal, so an empty or all-down scan is indistinguishable from a confirmed-clean network. For a production internal-exposure scanner this is a genuine operational hazard (false sense of "clean").

Line citations in the finding all check out (finish_plan 654-664, write_manifest 620-638, skip stages 729 and 753). Severity high is defensible given the security-ops misjudgment risk, though a case for medium exists since it is an observability gap rather than data loss/crash. worth_fixing: yes — a surgical fix (aggregate live-host and open-TCP/UDP-port counts from the per-batch discovery/identify XML and print a summary line in finish_plan, optionally a distinct status/exit when zero hosts up) is small, non-speculative, and directly improves production reliability of the result signal.

all.title: protocol_ports misclassifies a bare port that follows a U: token as UDP, silently dropping TCP ports and scanning them as UDP
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:327-347 (protocol_ports), called by auto_tcp_discovery_ports:350-357 and auto_udp_ports:360-365
all.evidence: protocol_ports keeps a sticky `current` protocol from the last `T:`/`U:` prefix. With `--ports U:53,80`: `protocol_ports('U:53,80','T') -> []` and `protocol_ports('U:53,80','U') -> ['53','80']` (verified). So auto_tcp_discovery_ports('U:53,80') -> '' and auto_udp_ports('U:53,80') -> 'U:53,80'. In execute_auto, auto_tcp_discovery_ports()=='' is falsy (713) so BOTH tcp_discovery and tcp_identify are skipped (732-733), and port 80 is scanned as a UDP port.
all.impact: A user who specifies mixed ports as `U:53,80` (intending 80 as TCP, a natural reading) gets: TCP scanning entirely skipped, and TCP port 80 probed over UDP where it will never respond. The user's TCP intent is silently lost with no error. nmap's own `-p` semantics also make a bare port after `U:` belong to UDP, so this is subtle, but the tool gives no warning that TCP ports were dropped.
all.proposed_fix: Reject or warn when ports_override contains a U:/T: prefix followed by bare (unprefixed) ports, or normalize by requiring every segment to carry its protocol prefix in mixed specs. At minimum, when auto_tcp_discovery_ports() returns '' because the override had no T-classified ports BUT the override is non-empty and contains digits, raise a clear error instead of silently skipping all TCP stages.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed by reading and executing the code. protocol_ports (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:327-347) carries a sticky `current` protocol from the last T:/U: prefix, so a bare port following a U: token is classified UDP. Verified empirically: protocol_ports('U:53,80','T')==[] and ('U:53,80','U')==['53','80']; auto_tcp_discovery_ports({'ports_override':'U:53,80'})=='' and auto_udp_ports==' U:53,80'. In execute_auto (713) the empty TCP spec is falsy, so the else at 731-735 appends tcp_discovery AND tcp_identify as SKIPPED, and port 80 is then scanned over UDP via auto_udp_ports. validate_ports/PORTS_RE (97,180-186) accept 'U:53,80' without complaint, and the only protocol_ports guard (551) fires solely under --tcp-only, so in the default auto workflow there is NO warning that all TCP scanning was dropped. The defect is real and reachable (--ports is free text in both CLI and GUI). I downgrade from high to medium for two reasons: (1) the classification is actually consistent with nmap's own -p semantics (bare port after U: is UDP), which the finding concedes, so it is not a misclassification vs nmap; (2) it requires a specific, atypical mixed-spec form. The genuinely defensible defect is the SILENT loss of all TCP coverage with no error in a security-exposure scanner. Surgical fix: when ports_override is non-empty but auto_tcp_discovery_ports() yields no TCP ports in the auto workflow, warn or error (generalize the existing line-551 guard beyond --tcp-only) rather than silently skipping both TCP stages.

all.title: validate_ports accepts malformed specs (lone 'T:'/'U:', empty/double commas) that flow straight into nmap -p as garbage
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:97 (PORTS_RE), 180-186 (validate_ports); used at build_base_flags:301-304 and create_plan:550
all.evidence: PORTS_RE = `^[0-9TUtu:,\-\s]+$` only checks the character class, not structure. Verified: validate_ports('U:')='U:', validate_ports('T:')='T:', validate_ports(',,')=',,', validate_ports('T:80,,U:53')='T:80,,U:53'. build_base_flags then emits e.g. `-p U:` (verified: ['-Pn','-sV','-T4','-p','U:']). Note also `-p U:` is paired with a profile that has no -sU, so nmap is given a UDP port spec with no UDP scan type.
all.impact: Typos like `U:`, `,,`, or `T:80,,U:53` pass validation and are handed to nmap, which will either error out (wasting a scan run / failing the whole plan) or behave unexpectedly. Validation gives false confidence that the port spec is well-formed when it only filters characters.
all.proposed_fix: Tighten validation to reject empty segments and dangling protocol prefixes: split on ',', require each non-empty segment to be a port/range optionally prefixed by exactly one T:/U:, and error on empty segments. Reuse the same parse logic that protocol_ports relies on so validation and consumption agree.

verdict.rationale:
CONFIRMED REAL by reading the code and running it. PORTS_RE = `^[0-9TUtu:,\-\s]+$` (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:97) is a character-class-only check; validate_ports (180-186) just runs PORTS_RE.match and returns ports.replace(" ", ""), so it never validates structure. Empirically verified the exact claims: validate_ports('U:')=='U:', ('T:')=='T:', (',,')==',,', ('T:80,,U:53')=='T:80,,U:53' (also accepts '-' and ':'). Downstream chain confirmed: in --workflow single, build_base_flags:301-304 takes ports=validate_ports(args.ports) and does flags.extend(["-p", ports]) -> verified build_base_flags emits e.g. ['-Pn','-sV','-T4','-p','U:'] and ['...','-p',',,']. build_command:417,423 passes plan["base_flags"] straight into the nmap argv, and execute_single:704 calls fail_plan on any non-zero rc, so a port spec nmap rejects (e.g. `-p ,,`) aborts the whole single-workflow plan. --workflow single (line 807) and --ports/-p (line 812) are real user-facing inputs, so this is reachable.

TWO corrections to the finding's framing (do not change is_real, but matter for the fix): (1) The garbage-to-nmap bug only fully manifests in --workflow single. In --workflow auto, ports are re-derived through protocol_ports (327) / auto_tcp_discovery_ports / auto_udp_ports, which skip empty segments and dangling T:/U: prefixes (verified: protocol_ports(',,','T')==[], auto_tcp(',,')=='' , protocol_ports('T:80,,U:53','T')==['80']). So auto self-heals; single does not. (2) The finding's note that '-p U: is paired with a profile that has no -sU' is correct for the basic profile, reinforcing that the spec is nonsensical, but the core defect is the validation gap itself.

This is a genuine correctness/UX defect: validation advertises that it filters malformed port specs (it raises a helpful Korean error for disallowed chars) but only filters characters, giving false confidence while letting structurally-broken specs reach nmap and (in single workflow) fail the entire run. It is distinct from ISSUE-001 (UDP-stage abort).

all.title: tcp_only_ports can reduce a port spec to empty, leaving a single-workflow profile with -p '' or no ports and no guard
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:275-285 (tcp_only_ports), 292-297 (build_base_flags --tcp-only branch)
all.evidence: tcp_only_ports('U:53') -> '' (verified). In build_base_flags, when --tcp-only and the profile has -p, line 297 does `flags[idx+1] = tcp_only_ports(flags[idx+1])`, which can set the port value to ''. The empty-string guard in create_plan (551-552: raise 'TCP만 옵션을 사용할 때는 TCP 포트를 지정해야 합니다') only fires for `args.workflow == 'auto'`; for `--workflow single --tcp-only --ports U:53` there is no such check, so build_base_flags yields `[..., '-p', '']`.
all.impact: In single workflow, `--tcp-only` combined with a UDP-only --ports (or a phase1 profile reduced to nothing) produces `-p ` with an empty value, which nmap rejects, failing the run. The auto-workflow guard exists but the single-workflow path is unguarded, so the same user mistake fails differently / less clearly.
all.proposed_fix: Move the 'TCP-only requires a TCP port' guard so it also covers single workflow (or apply it inside build_base_flags after tcp_only_ports returns ''), raising a clear ValueError instead of emitting an empty -p value.

verdict.rationale:
The finding's core mechanism is factually wrong. It claims that `--workflow single --tcp-only --ports U:53` makes build_base_flags emit `[..., '-p', '']` (empty -p value) via line 297 `flags[idx+1] = tcp_only_ports(flags[idx+1])`. I verified empirically and by reading the code that this does NOT happen.

Trace of build_base_flags (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:288-318):
- Line 294-297 only rewrites the PRESET's own -p. The only preset with -p is `phase1` whose spec is PRECISION_PORTS = "T:1-65535,U:..." (line 27/91), so tcp_only_ports() reduces it to "T:1-65535" (verified: tcp_only_ports(PRECISION)='T:1-65535'), never empty.
- More importantly, when --ports is supplied, line 301 `ports = validate_ports(args.ports)` returns a truthy value for "U:53" because PORTS_RE (line 97) permits U/:/digits (verified: validate_ports('U:53')='U:53'). So lines 302-304 strip ALL -p value flags (discarding whatever line 297 set) and append `-p U:53`.

Empirical output:
- phase1 --tcp-only --ports U:53 -> [..., '-p', 'U:53']  (NOT '')
- basic  --tcp-only --ports U:53 -> ['-Pn','-sV','-T4','-p','U:53']  (NOT '')
- phase1 --tcp-only (no ports)   -> [..., '-p', 'T:1-65535']  (NOT '')
- basic  --tcp-only (no ports)   -> ['-Pn','-sV','-T4']  (no -p; harmless, nmap default ports)

So there is no single-workflow path that yields an empty `-p` value. The finding's evidence statement ("build_base_flags yields [..., '-p', '']") is false, and the described impact (nmap rejects empty -p) never occurs.

A genuinely-different latent issue exists (--tcp-only with a UDP-only --ports produces `-p U:53` while -sU is stripped at line 293, which nmap rejects as 'UDP ports without -sU'; the auto guard at 551-552 catches this via protocol_ports(...,'T') being empty, but the single path is unguarded). However, that is a distinct defect with a distinct mechanism, not the empty-`-p` claim filed here, and it is a contrived user-error edge (asking for TCP-only while specifying only UDP ports) that nmap itself rejects with a clear message. Not worth a surgical fix as described, and the finding as written misdiagnoses the code.

all.title: open_ports_from_xml unions ports across all hosts in a batch, so tcp_identify cross-probes every discovered port against every live host
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:451-461 (open_ports_from_xml uses a single set over root.findall('.//port')); execute_auto:720,725 feeds union into run_nmap_stage(...,'tcp_identify', tcp_ports, live_hosts)
all.evidence: open_ports_from_xml collects into one `ports: set[int]` across ALL <port> elements regardless of which <host> they belong to. Verified with a 2-host discovery XML (host A open 22, host B open 3389): open_ports_from_xml -> [22,3389] and live_hosts -> ['10.0.0.1','10.0.0.2']. build_auto_flags(tcp_identify) then builds `-p T:22,3389` and execute_auto targets both live hosts, so host A is re-probed on 3389 and host B on 22.
all.impact: For multi-host batches (non-batched CIDR runs), the identify stage scans the cartesian product of (all open ports) x (all live hosts). Final per-host state is still correct (identify reports actual open/closed), so this is not a data-correctness bug, but it inflates identify scan time/load roughly proportional to host-count and pollutes per-host port lists with closed entries. With --batch-size>0 each host is its own batch so the effect is contained; the default non-batched path is where it bites.
all.proposed_fix: Either document/recommend --batch-size 1 for multi-host auto runs, or extract open ports per-host and run identify per host with that host's own port set. At minimum note this amplification so operators size batches accordingly.

verdict.rationale:
Verified in scanner/scanops_scanner.py. open_ports_from_xml (lines 450-461) collects every open <port> into a single `ports: set[int]` with no per-host grouping, returning sorted(ports). execute_auto feeds that union plus the full live_hosts list to tcp_identify: tcp_ports = open_ports_from_xml(discovery_xml, "tcp") (line 720), live_hosts = live_hosts_from_xml(discovery_xml) (line 722), run_nmap_stage(..., "tcp_identify", tcp_ports, live_hosts) (line 725). build_auto_flags builds one `-p T:<union>` (line 392) and build_command applies it to all scan_targets (lines 419-425), so every live host is probed against the union of all hosts' open ports (cartesian product). Default --batch-size is 0 (line 825) -> make_batches returns a single unexpanded batch (lines 231-233, 546), so the default non-batched path is exactly where this amplification bites; the finding's claim is accurate. This is NOT a data-correctness bug: nmap re-tests each port per host so final per-host state is right. One correction to the finding's impact: AUTO_TCP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS includes --open (line 65), so closed ports are suppressed from output -- the "pollutes per-host port lists with closed entries" half does not actually materialize; the only real effect is inflated identify scan time/load roughly proportional to host-count.

Not worth a surgical fix now: output is correct, --open already prevents the polluted-port-list symptom, and the proposed per-host fix (extract open ports per host, run identify once per host) is a non-trivial structural change that sacrifices nmap's --min-hostgroup parallelism and adds per-invocation overhead/serialization across N hosts -- it may not even be a net win, and trading a working code path for an unproven optimization violates surgical-change discipline. This is an efficiency/documentation note (recommend --batch-size for large multi-host runs), not a defect requiring code change. Severity low.

all.title: open_ports_from_xml counts only state=='open', excluding UDP 'open|filtered' — the dominant UDP result — so UDP discovery via this path silently finds nothing
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:454-455 (state check requires exactly 'open')
all.evidence: `if state is None or (state.get('state') or '').lower() != 'open': continue`. Verified: a UDP port with state='open|filtered' returns [] from open_ports_from_xml(...,'udp'). nmap reports the vast majority of UDP ports as 'open|filtered' because closed UDP ports are inferred from ICMP unreachables that are rate-limited.
all.impact: Currently low-impact because the auto workflow does not derive UDP ports from discovery XML (it uses a fixed UDP_DEFAULT_PORTS list), so this stays latent. But it is a correctness trap: any future use of open_ports_from_xml(...,'udp') (the function already takes a protocol arg and is exercised in tests at line 247) will under-report UDP exposure by ignoring open|filtered. The strict 'open'-only filter is wrong for UDP semantics.
all.proposed_fix: When protocol=='udp', also accept state in {'open','open|filtered'} (or make the accepted-states set protocol-aware). Add a test asserting UDP open|filtered is treated as a candidate open port.

verdict.rationale:
VERIFIED in code. open_ports_from_xml (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:454-455) requires state EXACTLY "open": `state = port.find("state"); if state is None or (state.get('state') or '').lower() != 'open': continue`. This is applied uniformly regardless of the protocol arg, so a UDP port with state="open|filtered" (the dominant nmap UDP result, since closed UDP relies on rate-limited ICMP unreachables) is dropped from open_ports_from_xml(path,"udp"). The protocol-strict filter is genuinely wrong for UDP semantics.

HOWEVER the finding itself concedes this is LATENT, and that is confirmed: the auto workflow NEVER derives UDP ports from discovery XML. Line 720 calls open_ports_from_xml(discovery_xml,"tcp") with "tcp" only; UDP ports come from auto_udp_ports() (lines 360-365) which returns the fixed UDP_DEFAULT_PORTS (line 26) or a user override, never from XML. The "udp" code path of open_ports_from_xml has NO live caller — it is exercised only by tests (test_standalone_scanner.py:247, test_runner.py:116), and those tests use state="open", so they neither fail nor expose the bug. So there is zero current impact on scanner correctness/reliability/UX.

worth_fixing=false: This is a real but dormant correctness trap with no production caller. Fixing it now is hardening a dead path for hypothetical future use — speculative per the "minimum code / no handling for scenarios that don't occur" guidance. It becomes worth fixing only if/when a UDP-discovery-from-XML path is actually introduced; the fix should accompany that change (with its own test), not precede it.

all.title: Standalone scanner enforces no SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE allowlist — a copy-and-run footgun that can scan out-of-org networks
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:163-177 (collect_targets/validate_targets) and create_plan:534-583; contrast backend/scanops/scanning/scope.py:57-93 (check_scope/check_raw_scope)
all.evidence: validate_targets only checks a character regex: `bad = [t for t in targets if not TARGET_RE.match(t)]` (line 175). There is zero reference to SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE anywhere in scanner/ (grep -i scope returns no matches in scanner/). The server, by contrast, gates every scan via check_scope(): `if not nets: return ... bad = [h for h in hosts if not _in_scope(h, nets)] ... raise ValueError("허용된 스캔 대역(scope) 밖의 대상입니다...")`. README.md:57-59 documents SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE as the guard against "오타·잘못 붙여넣은 사외 대역 스캔 사고" and explicitly tells operators to copy scanner/scanops_scanner.py to a scanner host (README.md:40, line 5-6 of the module docstring).
all.impact: The scanner is documented and designed to be copied standalone onto arbitrary hosts and run by auditors. A single typo (10.0.0.0/8 -> 100.0.0.0/8) or a pasted external CIDR causes an active SYN/UDP scan (AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS uses -sS -p T:1-65535 -T4 --max-parallelism 100) against an out-of-scope / third-party network with no guard whatsoever. The exact accident the server's scope gate was built to prevent is wide open in the standalone tool, and operators who trust the README's scope guarantee will assume they are protected when they are not.
all.proposed_fix: Port the scope logic: read an env var (e.g. SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE) in create_plan after collect_targets, and if set, expand targets and reject any host outside the allowed CIDRs (mirroring backend/scanops/scanning/scope.py:check_scope). At minimum honor it in the GUI too. Document in scanner/README that scope is NOT enforced unless the env var is set, so operators do not assume server-equivalent safety.

verdict.rationale: Verified against source. validate_targets (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:174-177) only does a character-class check: `bad = [t for t in targets if not TARGET_RE.match(t)]`, where TARGET_RE (line 96) = `^[A-Za-z0-9_.:/\-]+$`. This accepts any IP/CIDR/hostname-shaped token, so a typo like 100.0.0.0/8 or a pasted external CIDR passes. create_plan (534-583) and collect_targets (163-171) contain no allowlist logic; create_plan only does collect_targets -> expand_targets (a --max-hosts cap check, not scope). Grep for scope|SCAN_SCOPE|SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE across all of scanner/ returns zero matches (the only env access is the GUI copying os.environ for encoding at scanops_scanner_gui.py:354). By contrast the server gates every scan via backend/scanops/scanning/scope.py:57-69 check_scope (raises ValueError on out-of-scope hosts), and that module's docstring (lines 3-6) cites the exact 10.0->100.0 typo scenario. The standalone tool is explicitly documented/designed to be copied and run independently (module docstring scanner/scanops_scanner.py:4-6; README.md:40). README.md:57-59 documents SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE as the guard against scanning external ranges, in a general 보안/운영 section, with no caveat that it does not apply to the standalone scanner; scanner/README.md never mentions scope at all. The auto workflow fires an active full-range SYN scan (AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS lines 54-59: -sS -PE ... -p T:1-65535 -T4 --max-parallelism 100), so a mistyped/external CIDR launches an aggressive scan against a third party with no guard. This is a genuine safety gap, in scope for standalone-scanner reliability/safety. Minor: the finding's cited line range for validate_targets is loose (the regex check is precisely line 175, within the cited 163-177), but every load-bearing claim is confirmed. Distinct from ISSUE-001 (UDP-stage fail_plan). Worth a surgical fix: port check_scope into create_plan (and the GUI) reading SCANOPS_SCAN_SCOPE, and document in scanner/README that scope is not enforced unless the env var is set.

all.title: Auto workflow reports success (rc=0, GUI shows "완료") even when the scan found nothing importable
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:711-769 (execute_auto) + 654-664 (finish_plan); GUI status at scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:402
all.evidence: When discovery finds 0 open TCP ports and 0 live hosts, execute_auto takes only append_skipped_stage paths (lines 728-729 for tcp_identify, 753 for udp_identify), falls through to `finish_plan(...)` and `return 0` (lines 768-769). finish_plan only prints `done: {manifest_path}` (line 662). write_manifest then produces `import_xml_files = []` (lines 631-637) because every importable run was skipped. No code anywhere warns the user that the import list is empty. The GUI maps rc==0 to `self.status.set("완료")` (line 402), i.e. "complete/success".
all.impact: A scan of a dead subnet, a fully firewalled host, or a mistyped target completes and shows green "완료" / `done:` with an empty manifest. The operator believes the target is clean/has no exposure, when in reality discovery simply found nothing reachable. For an internal-exposure audit this is a dangerous false-negative presented as success — the user cannot distinguish "securely closed" from "never reached / wrong target".
all.proposed_fix: In finish_plan, count importable XML files (or open ports across runs) and print an explicit warning when zero, e.g. `warning: 가져올 결과가 없습니다 (열린 포트/살아있는 호스트 0). 대상/네트워크 도달성을 확인하세요.` The GUI can surface the same when import_xml_files is empty, distinct from a normal completion.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed in code. In execute_auto (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:711-769), when tcp_discovery finds 0 open TCP ports the tcp_identify stage is recorded via append_skipped_stage (lines 728-729), and when 0 live hosts are found udp_identify is skipped (line 753). append_skipped_stage marks these with returncode:0, skipped:True (lines 521-522). nmap exits 0 for a successfully-run scan that found nothing, so no fail_plan path triggers; execution falls through to finish_plan (line 768) and returns 0 (line 769). finish_plan only writes state and prints `done: {manifest_path}` (line 662) with no result-emptiness check. write_manifest's import_xml_files comprehension (lines 631-637) excludes skipped runs (line 634) and tcp_discovery, so it is [] when both identify stages are skipped. Grep confirms import_xml_files is never read back to warn, and no open-port/live-host-count warning string exists anywhere in scanner or GUI. The GUI maps rc==0 -> status "완료" at scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:402, the only status mapping, with no empty-result branch. Net effect: a dead subnet, fully-firewalled host, or mistyped target completes with green "완료"/`done:` and an empty manifest, indistinguishable from a securely-closed target — a false-negative presented as success for an internal-exposure audit. Distinct from ISSUE-001 (that is about aborting good results on stage failure; this is about successfully reporting an empty/unreachable scan as success).

all.title: GUI Stop on Windows force-kills the CLI (taskkill /F), so the scan's interrupted/resume state is never written and exit shows a generic error code
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:380-390 (_stop) vs scanner/scanops_scanner.py:787-792 (KeyboardInterrupt handler) and 780-781
all.evidence: _stop runs `subprocess.run(["taskkill", "/F", "/T", "/PID", str(self.proc.pid)])` (gui line 386) — a hard, unconditional kill, not Ctrl-C/CTRL_BREAK. The CLI's only graceful path is `except KeyboardInterrupt:` (scanner line 787) which sets `plan["status"] = "interrupted"`, writes state, and prints `"interrupted. Resume with: --resume " + str(state_path)` (line 791). taskkill /F never raises KeyboardInterrupt in the child, so that block does not run; the last persisted status is `"running"` (written at line 781 / after each stage). The killed Python process returns a nonzero code, which _drain_output renders as `self.status.set(f"종료 코드 {rc}")` and logs `exit code: {rc}` (gui lines 402-403).
all.impact: After the user clicks 중지 (Stop): (1) the state.json is stuck at status "running" — a zombie state the README claims the system avoids (README.md:62-63), so a later resume / inspection misrepresents what happened; (2) the user is never shown the resume hint that the CLI was designed to print, so the documented resume UX is silently lost on the most common (Windows) path; (3) the GUI shows a scary "종료 코드 1" (looks like a crash) instead of "중지됨". The GUI even created a CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP (gui line 367) enabling CTRL_BREAK, but _stop never uses it.
all.proposed_fix: Send a graceful signal first: on Windows `self.proc.send_signal(signal.CTRL_BREAK_EVENT)` (the process group flag is already set) and only taskkill /F as a timed fallback; on POSIX keep os.killpg with SIGINT (not SIGTERM) so the CLI's KeyboardInterrupt path runs. Also set a 'stopped' flag so _drain_output displays "중지됨" rather than "종료 코드 N".

verdict.rationale: Confirmed by reading the code. GUI _stop (scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:385-386) on Windows runs `subprocess.run(["taskkill","/F","/T","/PID",str(self.proc.pid)])` — a hard TerminateProcess that does NOT deliver Ctrl-C/CTRL_BREAK, so the child cannot raise KeyboardInterrupt. The CLI's ONLY graceful path is `except KeyboardInterrupt` (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:787-792); grep confirms NO signal.signal handler is installed in the CLI at all. Therefore on Stop: (a) plan["status"] stays "running" (last write at scanops_scanner.py:780-781 and per-stage write_json), never flipping to "interrupted"; (b) the designed resume hint print("\ninterrupted. Resume with: --resume "+state_path) at line 791 never fires; (c) _drain_output (gui:399-403) renders the nonzero exit as `종료 코드 {rc}`/`exit code: {rc}` with no "중지됨" distinction, looking like a crash. The project documents the opposite invariant in README.md:62-63 ("interrupted 로 정직하게 표기 ... 좀비 '실행 중' 방지"). Also confirmed CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP IS set at spawn (gui:367) enabling CTRL_BREAK_EVENT, yet _stop never uses it. ADDITIONAL grounded nuance the finding understated: this is cross-platform — POSIX _stop sends SIGTERM (gui:388), and the CLI has no SIGTERM handler, so the KeyboardInterrupt block is also skipped on Linux/macOS; the proposed POSIX fix (SIGINT instead of SIGTERM) is correct and necessary. Severity adjusted from high to medium: no scan data is actually lost (each stage's outputs are written by run_nmap_stage before Stop, and `cursor` is advanced + persisted, so --resume continues correctly regardless of the status string — load_plan at 586-598 doesn't gate on status), so impact is misleading zombie state + lost resume hint + scary exit-code UX rather than data loss or broken resume. Still worth a surgical fix (send CTRL_BREAK_EVENT first / SIGINT on POSIX, taskkill /F only as timed fallback, and a 'stopped' flag for the status label).

all.title: GUI Stop sends SIGTERM on POSIX, but the CLI only handles KeyboardInterrupt — same lost interrupted-state/resume-hint problem off Windows
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:388 (os.killpg(self.proc.pid, signal.SIGTERM)) vs scanner/scanops_scanner.py:787
all.evidence: The non-Windows branch does `os.killpg(self.proc.pid, signal.SIGTERM)` (gui line 388). The CLI installs no SIGTERM handler; its only cleanup is `except KeyboardInterrupt` (scanner line 787), which corresponds to SIGINT, not SIGTERM. Default SIGTERM disposition terminates the process without running the except block.
all.impact: On Linux/macOS scanner hosts, Stop leaves state.json at "running" and skips the resume-hint print, identical to the Windows defect. Since the README pitches the tool as Windows/Linux/macOS (README.md:41), this affects the documented cross-platform path.
all.proposed_fix: Either send signal.SIGINT instead of SIGTERM from the GUI, or register a SIGTERM handler in the CLI that raises KeyboardInterrupt / runs the same interrupted-state cleanup.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed from code. GUI _stop() POSIX branch sends os.killpg(self.proc.pid, signal.SIGTERM) (scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:388); the GUI launches the CLI with start_new_session=True (gui:369), so SIGTERM hits the CLI process group. The CLI installs NO signal handler: main() (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:833-843) and module top register no signal.signal(...), and the only interrupt cleanup is `except KeyboardInterrupt` at scanner:787, which Python raises only for SIGINT, not SIGTERM. Default SIGTERM disposition kills the Python process without running the except block, so on Linux/macOS Stop leaves state.json at status="running" (set at scanner:780-781, never updated to "interrupted") and never prints the resume hint (scanner:791). README.md:41 documents Windows/Linux/macOS support, so the POSIX path is on the advertised surface. This is the POSIX sibling of the Windows taskkill Stop bug (gui:386) and is distinct from ISSUE-001 (UDP-stage exit aborting the whole plan via fail_plan). Severity medium not high: --resume keys off cursor so scans are still resumable in practice; the harm is the stuck "running" status and missing resume hint, a UX/state-integrity defect rather than data loss. Fix is surgical: send signal.SIGINT instead of SIGTERM from the GUI, or register a SIGTERM->KeyboardInterrupt handler in the CLI.

all.title: Resume UX: the only resume hint goes to stderr from the CLI; the GUI gives no resume affordance after a failed/stopped scan and never auto-fills the state path
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:791 (stderr resume hint) and fail_plan:692-697 (no hint at all); GUI resume section scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:171-176, 259-262
all.evidence: On a stage failure the CLI calls fail_plan (scanner lines 692-697) which writes status "failed" and returns rc but prints NO resume guidance — only the KeyboardInterrupt path prints the `--resume` hint (line 791). In the GUI, resume requires the user to manually browse for `*.state.json` via _browse_resume (gui line 259) and click 재개 실행; nothing auto-populates self.resume_path after a run fails, and the GUI never echoes the state-file location (the CLI prints it only in the interrupt case, to stderr which is merged into the log).
all.impact: After the documented common failure (e.g. ISSUE-001's UDP-stage nonzero exit aborting the plan), the user sees `종료 코드 7` in the GUI with zero indication that the partial scan is resumable or where the state file is. The resume feature exists but is effectively undiscoverable at the moment it is needed, so users re-run the whole expensive full-port scan from scratch.
all.proposed_fix: Have fail_plan print the same `Resume with: --resume <state_path>` hint the interrupt path prints. In the GUI, on a nonzero/failed exit, auto-set self.resume_path to the just-run state.json (derivable from output_dir + name) and surface a one-click '재개' prompt.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed against the actual code on every claim. CLI: fail_plan (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:692-697) writes status "failed" + manifest and returns rc but prints NOTHING — no resume hint, no state path. The only place a resume hint is emitted is the KeyboardInterrupt branch at line 791 (`"\ninterrupted. Resume with: --resume " + str(state_path)`, to sys.stderr). I also verified the state path is never printed on any normal path: print_plan (601-617) prints only `output:`/`batches:`; finish_plan (662) prints `done: <manifest_path>`, not the state file. So after a stage failure (e.g. ISSUE-001's UDP nonzero exit), the user has no on-screen indication the partial scan is resumable or where the .state.json lives. GUI: resume is manual-only — _browse_resume (scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:259-262) + the 재개 실행 button (line 176). _drain_output's "done" handler (lines 398-403) on nonzero rc only sets status `종료 코드 {rc}` and logs `exit code: {rc}`; it never sets self.resume_path and never surfaces the state-file location. The GUI never derives the state path itself and only echoes CLI stdout/merged-stderr (stderr=STDOUT at line 359), which on failure contains no path. Net effect: the resume feature is undiscoverable exactly when it is needed, so users re-run the whole expensive full-port scan. This is a genuine UX defect that compounds the already-filed reliability issue. Severity medium: no data loss or crash (partial results ARE saved and resumable), but it defeats the value of an existing recovery feature. Worth fixing: the fix is surgical and grounded — add the same resume hint to fail_plan (mirror line 791), and in the GUI auto-populate self.resume_path on nonzero exit (path derivable from output_dir + name, set at scanner.py:562). Minor caveat on the proposed GUI fix: deriving the state path from output_dir+name requires the GUI to know the actual --name used (the CLI defaults to scan_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS when --name is blank, scanner.py:803), so the most robust GUI implementation is to have the CLI print the state path on failure and parse it from the log, or have the CLI emit the path on a known prefix — but the core finding stands regardless.

all.title: GUI never validates output name / batch-size numeric edge cases or surfaces CLI validation failures distinctly — silent acceptance of empty results from typos
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:325-329 (batch check) and _command 277-337; CLI argparse type=int at scanner/scanops_scanner.py:825-826
all.evidence: The GUI validates batch size only as `batch.isdigit()` (gui line 327), which rejects negatives by treating them as non-digit but gives the generic message "배치 크기는 숫자여야 합니다"; a value like `0` is allowed and silently means 'one nmap run' but the UI label '배치 크기' offers no hint of that meaning. More importantly, all other invalid inputs (bad ports, bad target tokens, max-hosts cap exceeded) are only caught by the CLI subprocess, whose `error: ...` text (scanner lines 838-843, rc=2) is dumped into the scrolling log as plain text with `exit code: 2`; there is no messagebox or status distinction, so a validation rejection looks the same as a generic failure.
all.impact: A user who fat-fingers a CIDR that expands beyond --max-hosts (default 65536) or an invalid port spec gets a Korean `error:` line buried in the log and a red-herring `종료 코드 2`, with no modal drawing attention. Combined with finding #2 (empty success), misconfigured runs are easy to misread as either success or an opaque crash.
all.proposed_fix: Detect rc==2 (CLI ValueError convention) in _drain_output and pop a messagebox with the captured error line, distinct from other failures. Add a tooltip/label clarifying batch size 0 = single run, and pre-validate ports/targets client-side before spawning.

verdict.rationale: Partially confirmed. The accurate core: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:399-403 (_drain_output) collapses every exit code into a single generic "종료 코드 {rc}" status and just _append_log's output as plain text, with NO messagebox for any failure. There is no distinction between rc=2 (CLI ValueError from invalid ports/targets/max-hosts cap) and rc=1 (OSError/runtime). Grounded: CLI main() lines 838-840 returns rc=2 on ValueError; ValueErrors are raised for bad target tokens (line 177), invalid ports (line 185), and max-hosts cap exceeded (line 226). So a config-validation rejection genuinely looks identical to a generic crash, surfaced only as a buried Korean "error:" log line. Also confirmed: batch size is validated only via isdigit() (gui line 327), and "0"/empty silently means a single nmap run (CLI default, line 825) with no UI hint. HOWEVER, the finding's headline claim is misframed and NOT real: the GUI not validating the output name does NOT cause "silent empty results from typos" — the CLI's safe_name (lines 128-130) sanitizes the name (regex substitution + fallback to scan_<timestamp>), so a bad name is silently cleaned, never empty or failing. Severity is low: the error text is always printed to the visible log, so nothing is truly hidden — the defect is only that attention isn't drawn and a validation rejection is indistinguishable from a crash.

all.title: udp_identify falling back to skipped (no live hosts) is reported identically to a real UDP scan with zero findings — control flow hides why UDP coverage was dropped
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:750-754 (live_hosts empty -> append_skipped_stage) vs 743-749 (--udp-all-targets actually scans)
all.evidence: In the default (non --udp-all-targets) path, if discovery returns no live hosts, udp_identify is recorded as skipped with reason "tcp_discovery 에서 살아있는 호스트를 찾지 못했습니다" (line 753) and the plan still finishes rc=0. The skip is only visible by reading state.json runs[].skip_reason — the console prints nothing about it during the run, and finish_plan emits only `done:`. A host that is silent to TCP/ICMP/ACK discovery but exposes UDP-only services (DNS/SNMP/NTP — exactly the kind of internal exposure this tool targets) is silently never UDP-scanned unless the user happened to know to pass --udp-all-targets.
all.impact: For internal exposure scanning, UDP-only hosts (open resolvers, SNMP, NTP amplifiers) are precisely the high-value findings, and they are dropped with no on-screen indication. The user sees a green completion and assumes UDP was covered; the coverage gap is buried in JSON. This is a silent false-negative in the tool's core use case.
all.proposed_fix: When udp_identify is skipped due to no live hosts (but UDP ports were configured), print a visible warning to stdout suggesting --udp-all-targets, and surface skipped-stage reasons in the GUI log/status rather than only in state.json.

verdict.rationale:
Verified against scanner/scanops_scanner.py and scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py. The defect is real and distinct from ISSUE-001.

Confirmed code path: in execute_auto, when discovery ran (auto_tcp_discovery_ports truthy) and live_hosts is empty, udp_identify is recorded as skipped at line 753 with reason "tcp_discovery 에서 살아있는 호스트를 찾지 못했습니다" via append_skipped_stage (lines 510-527), which writes ONLY to plan["runs"] in state.json and prints nothing. The plan then finishes rc=0 (finish_plan, line 707/768) which prints only "done:" (line 662). No console signal that UDP coverage was dropped.

Default-reachability confirmed: auto_udp_ports (line 360-365) returns the default UDP port set when no --ports override is given (UDP_DEFAULT_PORTS, line 26), so the elif chain reaches the live_hosts check (line 750-754) by default. The skip is thus the DEFAULT outcome whenever discovery finds zero live hosts — not an edge case. A host silent to TCP/ICMP/ACK discovery but exposing UDP-only services (DNS/SNMP/NTP) is never UDP-scanned unless the user passes --udp-all-targets (lines 743-749). This is a genuine silent false-negative in the tool's stated core use case (internal exposure scanning).

GUI half confirmed: _drain_output (gui 392-403) only echoes CLI stdout "line" events and sets status to "완료" on rc=0; it never reads state.json, so skip_reason is invisible in the GUI too (the only state.json reference is the resume-file picker, gui line 175/260).

One correction to the finding's framing: the TITLE overstates by saying the skip is "reported identically to a real UDP scan with zero findings." On the console they are NOT identical — a real UDP scan prints a "[x/y] 주요 UDP 서비스 식별 nmap ..." command line via run_nmap_stage (line 673), whereas the skip prints nothing. The skip is actually LESS visible (fully silent), which strengthens rather than weakens the underlying UX defect. The evidence/impact/proposed_fix fields are accurate.

Distinct from ISSUE-001 (whole-plan abort via fail_plan); this is about silent, by-design coverage drop with no user-facing signal. In scope for scanner UX/reliability. Worth a surgical fix: emit a stdout warning when udp_identify is skipped for no-live-hosts while UDP ports are configured (suggest --udp-all-targets), and surface skipped-stage reasons in the GUI log/status. Severity medium: data is preserved in state.json and the dropped coverage is a documented deliberate tradeoff (comments lines 751-754); the gap is purely missing signaling, not data loss.

all.title: GUI 'TCP만'/'단일 실행에 UDP 추가'/NSE toggles silently no-op or conflict depending on mode, with no feedback
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:154-158 (checks), 165-166 (NSE), _command:313-324; CLI semantics scanner/scanops_scanner.py:298 (args.udp only matters when -sU absent) and build_base_flags vs apply_auto_modifiers
all.evidence: The '단일 실행에 UDP 추가' checkbox label itself admits it only applies to single mode, but the UI still lets the user check it in auto mode where --udp is consumed by build_base_flags (only used for single profiles, scanner line 298) and ignored by the auto path (execute_auto/build_auto_flags never read plan['udp']/args.udp). Likewise '단일 실행에서 기본 NSE 추가' (nse_default, gui line 165) is meaningless in auto mode. open_only and include_closed can both be checked simultaneously (gui lines 156-157); the CLI applies include_closed (strip --open) then open_only (re-add --open) so include_closed silently wins/loses depending on order (scanner lines 314-317 / 376-379) with no warning.
all.impact: Users toggling these options in the wrong mode get no effect and no message, leading them to believe they constrained/expanded the scan when they did not (e.g. checking 'TCP만' is honored, but checking '단일 실행에 UDP 추가' in auto mode does nothing). Conflicting open/closed selections produce non-obvious behavior. This erodes trust and can cause an operator to ship a scan that doesn't match their intent.
all.proposed_fix: Disable/grey out mode-irrelevant checkboxes based on the selected run mode (auto vs single), and make open_only/include_closed mutually exclusive radio-style. Add a brief warning when a checked option will be ignored for the current mode.

verdict.rationale:
Verified against code. The core claim holds: the GUI never gates its checkboxes by run mode (`_mode_changed` gui:197-214 only updates label/port-preset text; the Checkbuttons at gui:154-158,165-166 have no state management), and several toggles are genuine silent no-ops in auto mode.

CONFIRMED no-ops in auto mode:
- `--udp` (self.udp): `_command` always appends `--udp` if checked (gui:315-316) regardless of mode. In the CLI `args.udp` is read ONLY in build_base_flags (scanner:298), which only feeds plan["base_flags"]. The auto path builds stage flags via build_auto_flags (used whenever stage_id is set, scanner:417) and applies apply_auto_modifiers (scanner:368-380), which never reads any udp key. So checking "단일 실행에 UDP 추가" in auto mode does nothing.
- `--nse-default` (self.nse_default, gui:165/317-318): args.nse_default is read only in build_base_flags (scanner:310); the auto path uses plan["scripts"] (apply_auto_modifiers scanner:374) and AUTO_*_FLAGS carry their own NSE, so nse_default is ignored in auto.
- open_only and include_closed can both be checked at once (gui:156-157, no mutual exclusion) and both flags are emitted (gui:321-324) with no warning.

There is no feedback anywhere when a checked option is irrelevant for the current mode. This can cause an operator to believe they expanded coverage (e.g. added UDP in auto) when they did not — a real UX/intent-correctness defect for a tool aimed at production internal-exposure scanning, distinct from ISSUE-001 (exit-code abort).

ONE INACCURACY in the finding to note: it claims include_closed "silently wins/loses depending on order." That is wrong. In both build_base_flags (scanner:314-317) and apply_auto_modifiers (scanner:376-379) the order is fixed in code: include_closed strips --open first, then open_only re-adds --open. So open_only deterministically wins regardless of checkbox order. It is still a real conflict (two contradictory options accepted, one silently overridden, no warning), just not order-dependent.

Net: real, in scope, low severity. A surgical fix (disable mode-irrelevant checkboxes per mode, make open-only/include-closed mutually exclusive, and/or warn when a checked option will be ignored) is justified for the production-reliability goal.

all.title: --scan-type connect still emits -sU in auto UDP stage, so the 'no privileges' choice fatally fails the UDP identify
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:267-272 (set_scan_type), :103 (SCAN_TYPE_FLAGS), :72-76 (AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS); GUI scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:48-52 (SCAN_TYPES label '권한 불필요 - TCP Connect')
all.evidence: SCAN_TYPE_FLAGS = {"-sS", "-sT"} and set_scan_type does `flags = [f for f in flags if f not in SCAN_TYPE_FLAGS]; return [mapped, *flags]`. It only ever strips/replaces -sS/-sT and never touches -sU. AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS begins with -sU. Dry-run with --scan-type connect produces: `-sT -sU -Pn -n -sV ... -p U:7,53,...`. -sU always requires raw-socket (root/admin) privileges; it cannot be performed via connect().
all.impact: A user who explicitly picks 'TCP Connect / no admin privileges required' in the GUI still gets a UDP identify stage that requires admin. On an unprivileged host nmap aborts the UDP stage with 'requires root privileges', which (per ISSUE-001's fail_plan path at :748) marks the whole plan failed. The user's deliberate privilege-free choice is silently violated and the scan dies.
all.proposed_fix: When scan_type=='connect' (no raw-socket privilege available), the UDP identify stage must be skipped with an explicit append_skipped_stage reason instead of emitting -sU. Either strip -sU and skip udp_identify when scan_type is connect, or surface an upfront error/warning that UDP scanning is unavailable without privileges.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed from code and empirical dry-run. set_scan_type (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:267-272) strips only SCAN_TYPE_FLAGS = {"-sS","-sT"} (:103) and never removes -sU. AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS (:72-76) starts with -sU. build_auto_flags(udp_identify) (:398) builds from those flags, then apply_auto_modifiers (:369) calls set_scan_type with plan scan_type. Running build_auto_flags({'scan_type':'connect'}, 'udp_identify') yields: -sT -sU -Pn -n -sV --open --reason -T4 --max-retries 2 --script ... -p U:7,53,... — i.e. -sU is still present alongside -sT. The GUI maps "권한 불필요 - TCP Connect" -> connect (gui :48-52) and passes --scan-type connect (gui :310-312), so this is reachable end-to-end. -sU requires raw-socket (root/admin) privileges and cannot run via connect(); on an unprivileged host nmap exits non-zero, which hits the fail_plan path for udp_identify (:748-749, :757-758, :762-763), aborting the whole plan and discarding successful TCP results. This is distinct from ISSUE-001: ISSUE-001 is the generic "UDP exit!=0 -> fail_plan" cascade; this finding is that the user's explicit no-privileges choice is silently violated by still emitting -sU, which deterministically guarantees that failure for exactly the users who opted out of needing admin. Proposed fix is sound: when scan_type=='connect', skip udp_identify with append_skipped_stage (mirroring the existing tcp_only skip at :737-739) rather than emitting an -sU command that cannot succeed.

all.title: Auto discovery stage keeps raw-socket-only probes (-PE/-PA/--defeat-rst-ratelimit) even under --scan-type connect
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:49-59 (AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS: -PE, DISCOVERY_PA='-PA80,443,3389', --defeat-rst-ratelimit), applied via build_auto_flags:384-388 then apply_auto_modifiers/set_scan_type
all.evidence: Dry-run with --scan-type connect: `-sT -PE -PS21,22,... -PA80,443,3389 -n -T4 --reason ... --defeat-rst-ratelimit ...`. set_scan_type swaps -sS->-sT but leaves -PE (ICMP echo), -PA (raw TCP ACK ping) and --defeat-rst-ratelimit untouched. These probe types require raw sockets; unprivileged nmap cannot send raw ICMP/ACK and downgrades or warns, and --defeat-rst-ratelimit has no effect without raw sockets.
all.impact: The 'no privileges' path is not actually privilege-clean: discovery loses its ICMP-echo and ACK reachability probes when run unprivileged, so hosts that answer only ICMP or only ACK (the exact cases the -PE/-PA combo at :47-48 was added to catch) are missed, and discovery silently behaves differently than its documented design. Coverage degrades without any signal to the operator.
all.proposed_fix: Tie raw-socket probe flags to privilege availability: when scan_type=='connect', build a connect-compatible discovery probe set (e.g. -PS only, drop -PE/-PA/--defeat-rst-ratelimit) and note in preview/log that ICMP/ACK discovery is unavailable, or warn that auto discovery assumes privileged scanning.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed real by reading the code and by an empirical dry-run. set_scan_type (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:267-272) only filters SCAN_TYPE_FLAGS={"-sS","-sT"} (line 103) and prepends the mapped type; it never strips raw-socket-only probes. AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS (lines 54-59) hardcodes -PE, DISCOVERY_PA="-PA80,443,3389" (line 50), and --defeat-rst-ratelimit. Flow: build_auto_flags (383-401) -> apply_auto_modifiers (368-380) -> set_scan_type (369). Dry-run `python scanops_scanner.py --workflow auto --scan-type connect --dry-run` emits the tcp_discovery line exactly as quoted: `-sT -PE -PS21,22,... -PA80,443,3389 -n -T4 --reason ... --defeat-rst-ratelimit ...` — so -sS->-sT is swapped but -PE/-PA/--defeat-rst-ratelimit remain. nmap-behavior claims are accurate: -PE (raw ICMP echo) and -PA (raw TCP ACK ping) require raw-socket privileges and are skipped/downgraded when unprivileged; --defeat-rst-ratelimit only affects raw-socket scanning. Result: under connect mode the discovery loses its ICMP-echo and ACK arms — exactly the cases the comment at lines 47-48 says -PE/-PA were added to catch (SYN-silent but ICMP/ACK-answering hosts) — degrading coverage with no signal from the tool. The GUI sharpens the contradiction: SCAN_TYPES labels connect as "권한 불필요 - TCP Connect" ("no privileges needed", scanops_scanner_gui.py:48-52, passed via --scan-type at 310-312), yet that very path emits privilege-requiring probes. Distinct from ISSUE-001 (UDP exit-code aborts plan). I moderated severity from the proposed medium to low: -PS (connect-compatible ping) still works so discovery is not fully broken — only the additive ICMP/ACK arms are lost — and nmap itself warns at its own layer; the defect is degraded coverage of a minority host class plus a broken "no privileges" promise, not a crash or wholesale wrong result. Worth fixing surgically: when scan_type=='connect', strip -PE/-PA*/--defeat-rst-ratelimit from the discovery probe set and note in preview/log that ICMP/ACK discovery is unavailable. Side observation (out of scope for this finding, separate dedup): the same set_scan_type prepend causes the udp_identify stage to emit `-sT -sU` together under connect mode.

all.title: phase1 'precision' single-run preset reintroduces the fatal -sU + --version-all combo the auto workflow was redesigned to avoid
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:83-94 (PRESETS['phase1']), :26-27 (UDP_DEFAULT_PORTS / PRECISION_PORTS); exposed via GUI scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:29 ('single_precision' -> 'phase1'), :36 description '전체 포트 정밀 확인'
all.evidence: phase1 = ["-sS", "-sU", "-Pn", "-n", "-sV", "--version-all", "--open", ... "-p", PRECISION_PORTS ...] with PRECISION_PORTS = "T:1-65535,U:7,53,...,161,...,1900,5353,5355,11211". This applies -sU with --version-all (intensity 9) to chatty/amplification UDP ports (53 DNS, 161 SNMP, 1900 SSDP, 5353 mDNS). The code's own comments at :70-71 state exactly this is dangerous: '--version-all ... 수다스러운/증폭형 UDP 서비스(SNMP·SSDP·DNS 등)에서 ... nmap 을 fatal 종료시킬 위험이 크고' — which is why AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS (:72-76) deliberately omits --version-all.
all.impact: The GUI 'single - 전체 포트 정밀 확인' (precision) mode and CLI --workflow single --profile phase1 can trigger the same nmap fatal exit on noisy/amplification UDP responses that ISSUE-001's redesign was built to prevent. Users choosing the 'most thorough' option get the least reliable, crash-prone scan. The protection added to auto was never propagated to the equivalent single preset.
all.proposed_fix: Remove --version-all from PRESETS['phase1'] (rely on default -sV intensity 7 for the UDP portion), or split phase1 so --version-all applies only to the TCP port range and not to -sU/U: ports — mirroring the auto stages' separation.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed from code. PRESETS['phase1'] (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:87-93) contains both "-sU" and "--version-all" together with "-p", PRECISION_PORTS. PRECISION_PORTS (line 27) = f"T:1-65535,U:{UDP_DEFAULT_PORTS}", and UDP_DEFAULT_PORTS (line 26) includes the exact chatty/amplification UDP ports the finding names (53 DNS, 161 SNMP, 1900 SSDP, 5353/5355 mDNS/LLMNR, 11211 memcached). The module's OWN comment at lines 70-71 states --version-all (intensity 9) on chatty/amplification UDP services (SNMP/SSDP/DNS) risks fatally terminating nmap, which is precisely why AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS (72-76) deliberately omits --version-all. That protection is NOT propagated to phase1. The flow is grounded: build_base_flags (line 289) copies PRESETS[profile] verbatim and never strips --version-all (only -sU is stripped, and only under --tcp-only at line 293); build_command for the single workflow (line 417, empty stage_id) emits plan["base_flags"] directly into the nmap command (lines 420-426). GUI mapping confirmed: scanops_scanner_gui.py:29 maps single_precision -> phase1, and lines 22/36 label/describe it as the most-thorough "전체 포트 정밀 확인" option. Default GUI scan type is "프로필 기본" so -sU is retained by default, meaning the -sU + --version-all combo reaches nmap unmodified. This is distinct from ISSUE-001 (which concerns fail_plan aborting the plan on UDP stage exit!=0): this finding is the fatal nmap exit itself being reintroduced in the single/phase1 code path that the auto redesign never touched. Real, in-scope reliability defect.

all.title: Dry-run preview shows UDP identify against original target, but a real run scopes it to discovery live-hosts (or skips) — preview misrepresents what runs
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:601-617 (print_plan / build_command with no targets), vs execute_auto:750-763 (UDP targets = live_hosts only) and :740-742/:752-754 (skip when no UDP ports / no live hosts)
all.evidence: print_plan calls build_command(plan, idx, "udp_identify") with no targets, so build_command (:418-419) falls back to `plan['batches'][index]` and the preview prints the UDP command with the original target appended (dry-run: `... -p U:7,53,... -oA ...udp_identify 10.0.0.5`). In a real run, execute_auto passes targets=live_hosts (:756) so the UDP scan actually runs against only discovery-confirmed hosts, or is skipped entirely (:753) if discovery found no live host. The preview never reflects this scoping or the possible skip.
all.impact: The GUI '명령 확인' (preview) and CLI --dry-run promise to show the commands that will run, but the previewed UDP target set and the real one differ. An operator validating scope/targets before a sensitive internal scan is shown commands that hit the original target, while the real run may hit a different (live-only) host set or skip UDP altogether. tcp_identify has the same preview/real divergence (preview shows original target; real run uses live_hosts at :725).
all.proposed_fix: Make print_plan annotate identify/UDP commands the way it already annotates discovered ports ('T:<open TCP ports from previous step>'), e.g. append a '<live hosts from discovery>' marker and a note that the stage may be skipped when discovery finds no live host or no UDP ports.

verdict.rationale:
VERIFIED REAL but lower severity than claimed (medium -> low), and not worth fixing now.

Mechanism confirmed in scanner/scanops_scanner.py:
- print_plan previews udp_identify via build_command(plan, idx, "udp_identify") with NO targets (line 615). build_command then falls back to the original batch: `scan_targets = targets if targets else plan["batches"][index]` (line 419). So the previewed UDP command appends the ORIGINAL target(s).
- In a real auto run, execute_auto scopes UDP to discovery survivors: run_nmap_stage(..., "udp_identify", targets=live_hosts) (line 756), or SKIPS entirely when discovery found no live host (lines 752-754 append_skipped_stage) — neither of which the preview shows.
- Same divergence for tcp_identify: preview at line 610 uses no targets -> original batch; real run uses live_hosts at line 725.
- GUI confirms operator impact: the "명령 확인" button (scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:187-188) calls _start(dry_run=True), which appends --dry-run (gui line 290), and CLI --dry-run routes to print_plan (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:773-775). So the GUI preview IS the print_plan output.
- The proposed-fix precedent is real: print_plan already annotates the derived-ports divergence, post-processing the tcp_identify preview with .replace("T:0", "T:<open TCP ports from previous step>") (line 610).

Why severity is LOW, not medium: the divergence is conservative, not deceptive about scope expansion. The previewed host set is the ORIGINAL batch the operator themselves typed, and the real run only ever scans a SUBSET of it (live_hosts) or skips — it never scans a host outside what the operator specified. So an operator validating scope before a sensitive scan is never shown fewer hosts than will actually be hit; the preview over-states, never under-states, the host set. The literal nmap flags/ports shown are accurate. The genuine gap is purely informational: the preview doesn't disclose (a) later stages narrow to discovery-live hosts and (b) UDP/tcp_identify may be skipped. The existing "T:<open TCP ports from previous step>" annotation already signals to the operator that later stages are derived from earlier results.

Why NOT worth fixing now: this is a preview-fidelity/clarity polish, not a correctness, safety, or reliability defect — no wrong, dangerous, or out-of-scope scan ever results. It is strictly dominated in priority by genuine reliability bugs in this scanner (e.g. the ISSUE-001 class of fail_plan discarding good results). The fix is small but speculative gold-plating against the audit's production-reliability goal; defer it.

all.title: Default auto workflow silently drops UDP-only hosts; GUI gives no way to enable full UDP coverage despite promising 'UDP service identification'
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:743-758 (udp_all_targets opt-in vs live_hosts-only default), :817-818 (--udp-all-targets help); GUI scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py (no --udp-all-targets control; README/GUI promise at :201-203 and README.md:58 '주요 UDP 서비스 ... 확인')
all.evidence: In execute_auto the default branch (:750-756) runs UDP identify only against live_hosts derived from TCP discovery (open_ports/PE/PS/PA). A host that is silent to TCP SYN, ICMP echo, and ACK-on-80/443/3389 but serves UDP (DNS/SNMP/NTP-only host) is never in live_hosts, so UDP identify excludes it (:752-754 even skips with '살아있는 호스트를 찾지 못했습니다'). Full coverage requires plan['udp_all_targets'] (:743), set only by --udp-all-targets. grep of scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py for udp_all_targets/--udp-all-targets returns no matches — the GUI never exposes it.
all.impact: The headline auto-scan feature ('주요 UDP 서비스 식별', and GUI expected-results text claiming UDP services are checked) silently misses UDP-only hosts for every GUI user, and for CLI users who don't know the buried --udp-all-targets flag. For internal exposure scanning, UDP-only assets (DNS/SNMP/SSDP/IKE) are exactly high-value findings, so the default produces false-negative coverage with no warning.
all.proposed_fix: Expose --udp-all-targets as a GUI checkbox (e.g. '숨은 UDP 전용 호스트도 확인'), and/or document clearly in the preview/log that default UDP identify only covers TCP/ICMP/ACK-discovered hosts so UDP-only hosts are skipped unless the option is enabled.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed by reading the code. In execute_auto (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:743-758), when the default tcp_discovery runs (auto_tcp_discovery_ports returns 'T:1-65535' by default, :350-357) and plan['udp_all_targets'] is falsy, the UDP identify stage is restricted to live_hosts derived solely from the TCP discovery XML (:719-722 + live_hosts_from_xml :464-487, which keeps only hosts with status state="up"). A host that responds only on UDP and stays silent to the TCP discovery probes never appears in live_hosts, so UDP identify either targets only TCP-discovered hosts (:756) or is skipped entirely with "tcp_discovery 에서 살아있는 호스트를 찾지 못했습니다." (:752-754). Full coverage requires the opt-in plan['udp_all_targets'] branch (:743-749), set only by --udp-all-targets (:572, :817-818). The GUI never exposes this: grep for udp_all/all-targets/all_targets in scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py returns 0 matches, and the command builder (gui:295-337) emits --tcp-only and --udp but never --udp-all-targets. Meanwhile the GUI auto-mode promise text (gui:34, :201-203) states "주요 UDP 서비스도 확인합니다" with no caveat, so every GUI user gets silent false-negative UDP-only-host coverage. This is genuinely distinct from ISSUE-001 (UDP exit!=0 aborting the whole plan). For internal exposure scanning, UDP-only assets (DNS/SNMP/SSDP/IKE) are high-value, making the silent gap meaningful. Severity medium (silent coverage gap on the headline feature, not a crash/data loss; CLI workaround exists). Worth fixing: the proposed fix (GUI checkbox to set --udp-all-targets, plus a clearer preview/log note that default UDP identify only covers TCP/ICMP/ACK-discovered hosts) is surgical and well-scoped.

all.title: CIDR/range cap is checked AFTER full materialization, so a large network (IPv4 /8 or any IPv6 CIDR) hangs/OOMs before the cap can fire
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:205-227 (expand_targets); reached unconditionally from create_plan at line 545
all.evidence: Line 217 `hosts.extend(str(ip) for ip in net)` fully materializes the entire network into a Python list, and only THEN line 225 `if len(hosts) > cap:` raises. The cap check is per-target, applied after each target is fully expanded, so it cannot interrupt expansion WITHIN one oversized target. Verified: `expand_targets(['10.0.0.0/8'], cap=1000)` (16.7M hosts) did not return within a 10s timeout; `expand_targets(['2001:db8::/96'], cap=10)` (4.29e9 hosts) hung past 8s; `expand_targets(['2001:db8::/64'], ...)` would attempt 1.8e19 addresses. create_plan calls `expand_targets(raw_targets, args.max_hosts)` (line 545) unconditionally even in non-batched mode, so a single mistyped /8 or any IPv6 CIDR freezes the process / exhausts memory before any scan starts.
all.impact: The --max-hosts safety valve is defeated by exactly the input it exists to guard against. A typo like `10.0.0.0/8` instead of `/24`, or any IPv6 CIDR, makes the scanner hang or get OOM-killed before printing anything — looks like a freeze to the operator, with no error and no scan.
all.proposed_fix: Before materializing, check `net.num_addresses > cap` (cheap, no expansion) and raise the same ValueError. When expanding, count incrementally and break/raise as soon as `len(hosts) > cap` is reached mid-network (e.g. iterate with a running counter or `itertools.islice(net, cap+1)`), instead of `hosts.extend(... for ip in net)`.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed from scanner/scanops_scanner.py. expand_targets (lines 205-227) materializes an entire CIDR via `hosts.extend(str(ip) for ip in net)` at line 217 and only checks the cap at line 225 (`if len(hosts) > cap`). The check is per-target, applied AFTER each target is fully expanded, so it cannot interrupt expansion within a single oversized CIDR. create_plan calls `expand_targets(raw_targets, args.max_hosts)` at line 545 unconditionally, BEFORE the `args.batch_size > 0` gate at line 546, so the materialization happens even in default non-batched mode (batch_size default 0, max_hosts default 65536). Empirically reproduced: `expand_targets(['10.0.0.0/8'], 1000)` (16.7M hosts) hangs past an 8s timeout (process exit 124) instead of immediately raising the cap ValueError; ipaddress confirms /8 = 16,777,216 addresses and v6 /96 = 4.29e9. The IPv4 RANGE_RE path (line 218-222) is safe because line 220 caps `hi > 255`, but the CIDR path has no size guard. Net effect: the --max-hosts safety valve is defeated by exactly the oversized input it exists to guard against; a typo like /8 or any large IPv6 CIDR freezes/OOMs the process with no output before any scan starts. This is distinct from ISSUE-001 (UDP stage failure aborting the plan). Severity high rather than critical: it requires operator misinput (an oversized CIDR), is recoverable by killing the process, and corrupts no results — but it is a real, silent denial-of-service of the tool. Proposed fix is sound and cheap: pre-check `net.num_addresses > cap` (arithmetic, instant) before materializing, and/or count incrementally / use itertools.islice(net, cap+1) to break mid-network.

all.title: Non-ValueError/OSError exceptions (KeyError from malformed state file on --resume) escape main() as a raw traceback
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:833-843 (main) and 586-598 (load_plan), 601-602 (print_plan)
all.evidence: main() catches only `except ValueError` and `except OSError`. load_plan (line 588) does `json.loads(...)` then only validates `plan.get('tool')`; it does not validate the schema. A valid-JSON state file that is missing required keys (output_dir, batches, cursor, base_flags, ...) passes load_plan and then KeyErrors deep inside execute/print_plan. Verified: a state file `{"tool":"scanops_scanner","nmap":"nmap"}` run with `--resume ... --dry-run` produces `KeyError: 'output_dir'` with a full traceback and exit code 1 (raw crash, not the clean `error: ...` path).
all.impact: A truncated/partially-written/older-format/hand-edited state.json gives the operator a Python traceback instead of a clean error message. In the GUI this dumps a stack trace into the run log. KeyError is the realistic case; any other non-ValueError/OSError raised inside execute() (e.g. a future code path) would similarly escape unfiltered.
all.proposed_fix: Either validate the state-file shape in load_plan (check for required keys, raise ValueError with a clear message on missing keys) or broaden main()'s handler with a final `except Exception as exc:` that prints `error: ...` and returns a nonzero code, while keeping KeyboardInterrupt handling intact.

verdict.rationale: Confirmed by reading the code AND reproducing it. main() (scanner/scanops_scanner.py:835-843) only catches `except ValueError` (returns 2) and `except OSError` (returns 1). load_plan (line 586-598) does json.loads then validates ONLY plan.get("tool") == "scanops_scanner" (line 589); it performs no schema validation of required keys. A valid-JSON state file missing required keys passes load_plan and then KeyErrors deep inside execute/print_plan. Empirically reproduced: `{"tool":"scanops_scanner","nmap":"nmap"}` run with `--resume <file> --dry-run` produces a full Python traceback ending in `KeyError: 'output_dir'` at print_plan line 602 (print(f"output: {plan['output_dir']}")), exit code 1 — the raw-crash path, not the clean `error: ...` handler. This is a realistic operational case for the resume feature: a truncated/partially-written/older-format/hand-edited state.json gives the operator a stack trace. Confirmed GUI impact: scanops_scanner_gui.py line 359 sets stderr=subprocess.STDOUT and appends subprocess output to the run log (lines 348/397/403), so the traceback is dumped verbatim into the GUI run log. Distinct from ISSUE-001 (which concerns UDP stage exit!=0 triggering fail_plan and discarding TCP results); this is about unhandled non-ValueError/OSError exceptions escaping main(). Proposed fix (validate required keys in load_plan and raise ValueError with a clear message, or add a final `except Exception` in main() that prints `error: ...` and returns nonzero while preserving the existing KeyboardInterrupt handling in execute) is surgical and justified for a tool meant to be production-reliable.

all.title: IPv6 targets are accepted and expanded but the scan never gets -6, so nmap aborts (and in auto workflow aborts the whole plan)
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner.py:96 (TARGET_RE), 211-217 (expand_targets CIDR branch), 464-487 (live_hosts_from_xml), AUTO_*_FLAGS at 54-76 (no -6 anywhere)
all.evidence: TARGET_RE = `^[A-Za-z0-9_.:/\-]+$` includes `:` so `2001:db8::1` and `2001:db8::/64` pass validate_targets. expand_targets handles IPv6 networks via ipaddress.ip_network (line 213). live_hosts_from_xml re-validates extracted addrs with TARGET_RE (line 484), which also passes IPv6. But grep for `-6` across the file finds zero scan-flag usages — none of AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS / AUTO_TCP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS / AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS / PRESETS includes `-6`. nmap requires `-6` to scan IPv6 and errors out ("... you must specify -6" / cannot mix address families) when given an IPv6 target without it.
all.impact: Any IPv6 internal target is silently accepted at the CLI/GUI layer, then nmap fails at runtime. In the default auto workflow, the tcp_discovery stage exits nonzero, which (per the existing fail_plan behavior, ISSUE-001) aborts the whole plan. Operators auditing IPv6-enabled internal networks get no results and a confusing failure rather than an upfront 'IPv6 not supported' message.
all.proposed_fix: Decide on support: either reject IPv6 targets early in validate_targets with a clear message, or detect IPv6 targets and inject `-6` into the nmap flags (noting nmap cannot mix IPv4/IPv6 in one invocation, so mixed batches must be split by family).

verdict.rationale: Confirmed by reading scanner/scanops_scanner.py. TARGET_RE at line 96 (`^[A-Za-z0-9_.:/\-]+$`) includes `:`, so IPv6 literals/CIDRs pass validate_targets (174-177). expand_targets (211-217) expands IPv6 networks via ipaddress.ip_network. live_hosts_from_xml re-validates with TARGET_RE (484), which also passes IPv6. No `-6` exists anywhere: grep finds only `T:1-65535` substrings; AUTO_TCP_DISCOVERY_FLAGS (54-59), AUTO_TCP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS (64-67), AUTO_UDP_IDENTIFY_FLAGS (72-76), and PRESETS (83-94) contain no `-6`. build_command (414-426) passes `*scan_targets` straight to nmap with no address-family detection; build_auto_flags (383-401) and apply_auto_modifiers (368-380) never inject `-6`. The GUI delegates target validation/scanning entirely to the CLI (no `-6` there either). So an IPv6 target is silently accepted then nmap errors at runtime ('you must specify -6'); in the auto workflow this hits the first stage (tcp_discovery) and, combined with the known fail_plan abort (ISSUE-001), discards everything. Distinct defect from ISSUE-001 (acceptance/UX gap, not the abort mechanism). Severity medium not high: failure is a clear nmap error rather than silent wrong results, and it only triggers when operators actually feed IPv6 targets (many internal nets are IPv4-only). Surgical fix: reject IPv6 early in validate_targets (and IPv6 CIDR in expand_targets) with a clear 'IPv6 not supported' message; full -6 support would require splitting batches by family.

all.title: GUI Stop on Windows hard-kills the tree (taskkill /F /T); CLI never records the killed stage and state stays "running" forever — CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP is set but never used for graceful stop
all.location: scanner/scanops_scanner_gui.py:366-367, 380-390 (_stop); scanner/scanops_scanner.py:780 (status=running), 787-792 (KeyboardInterrupt handler), 674-688 (run_nmap_stage)
all.evidence: GUI launches with `creationflags=CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP` (line 367) — the documented purpose of which is to allow sending CTRL_BREAK_EVENT for a graceful stop — but _stop() on Windows instead runs `taskkill /F /T /PID` (line 386), a forced kill. The CLI's only graceful-stop path is `except KeyboardInterrupt` (line 787) which writes status="interrupted". taskkill /F does not raise KeyboardInterrupt, so that handler never runs. The CLI process is killed mid `subprocess.call` (line 674) before `plan['runs'].append(run)` (line 687) and before any status update, leaving the last-persisted status as "running" (set line 780). On POSIX the same _stop sends SIGTERM via killpg (line 388) which also does not trigger the KeyboardInterrupt handler (only SIGINT would).
all.impact: After clicking Stop, the on-disk state.json is permanently stuck at "running" (never "interrupted"), so the file misrepresents what happened and any tooling/operator keying off status sees a 'still running' scan that is dead. The in-flight stage's partial -oA outputs are left on disk but never recorded in runs[]. (Resume itself still works because stage_succeeded only trusts returncode==0 records, so the killed stage re-runs.)
all.proposed_fix: For graceful stop on Windows, send `CTRL_BREAK_EVENT` to the process group (which the CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP flag was added for) so the CLI's KeyboardInterrupt path runs and writes status="interrupted", falling back to taskkill only after a timeout. On POSIX, send SIGINT (not SIGTERM) so the same handler fires. Alternatively, have the CLI install a SIGTERM/CTRL_BREAK handler that mirrors the KeyboardInterrupt cleanup.

verdict.rationale: Verified against the actual code. GUI _run_process sets creationflags=CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP (scanops_scanner_gui.py:367) but _stop never uses it for a graceful CTRL_BREAK_EVENT: on Windows it runs `taskkill /F /T /PID` (line 386, a TerminateProcess hard-kill) and on POSIX it sends SIGTERM via os.killpg (line 388). The CLI's only graceful-stop path is `except KeyboardInterrupt` which writes status="interrupted" (scanops_scanner.py:787-790). Python raises KeyboardInterrupt only on SIGINT by default; taskkill /F (TerminateProcess) and SIGTERM do not raise it, and grep confirms the CLI registers no signal.signal/atexit handler (signal is imported only in the GUI). So after Stop the last persisted status remains "running" (set at line 780), permanently misrepresenting a dead scan as in-flight to any tooling/operator keying off status. Also, run_nmap_stage uses subprocess.call (line 674) and only does plan["runs"].append(run) + write_json after it returns (lines 687-688); a mid-call kill records nothing for the in-flight stage even though its partial -oA files remain on disk. The finding's resume parenthetical is also correct: stage_succeeded requires returncode==0 (line 497), so the unrecorded killed stage simply re-runs on resume — i.e., no scan-result data loss, but persisted state is wrong. Distinct from ISSUE-001 (which is about UDP exit!=0 -> fail_plan discarding TCP results). Medium severity and a surgical fix (graceful CTRL_BREAK_EVENT on Windows / SIGINT on POSIX before falling back to taskkill, or a CLI SIGTERM/CTRL_BREAK handler mirroring the KeyboardInterrupt cleanup) are justified.

all.title: IP range/CIDR validation gaps: base octets >255 accepted, invalid CIDR silently passed through verbatim to nmap
